void.text: void: absence as substance, potential space

force.text: force: capacity for change, push, potential

containment.text: containment: inside and outside, limits and boundaries

temporal_flow.text: temporal flow: how time passes, duration

interoception.text: interoception: internal state of the body

mortal_observer.text: mortal observer: observer bound to time

eternal_observer.text: eternal observer: observer outside of time

creator_observer.text: creator observer: that creates, actively produces

attraction.text: attraction: tendency of approach, positive valence

decay.text: decay: progressive deterioration, dissolution of organized structure

aversion.text: aversion: tendency of avoidance, negative valence

cooperation.text: cooperation: joint action toward shared goal

loss.text: loss: absence after having had, void left by destruction

attention.text: attention: selective focus of consciousness, perceptual filter

intention.text: intention: directed purpose, will to act toward an end

stillness.text: stillness: absence of displacement, staying in position

01_mathematics_algebra_axiom_1_1.text: Closure: Combining any two elements of the group produces another element of the group.

01_mathematics_algebra_axiom_1_2.text: Associativity: The order in which you perform the operations doesn't matter (as long as you don't change the sequence of elements).

01_mathematics_algebra_axiom_1_3.text: Identity Element: There is a "do nothing" element that leaves everything unchanged.

01_mathematics_algebra_axiom_1_4.text: Inverse Element: Every action can be undone.

01_mathematics_algebra_axiom_2_3.text: Distributive laws: Multiplication distributes over addition — the link between the two operations.

01_mathematics_algebra_axiom_3_4.text: 0 ≠ 1: A field is a system where you can add, subtract, multiply, and divide (except by zero), and both addition and multiplication are commutative.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_1.text: The Homomorphism Principle: The "right" way to relate two algebraic structures is through maps that respect the operations.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_2.text: The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: The complex numbers are "complete" for polynomial equations — every polynomial equation has a solution in ℂ.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_3.text: Noether's Isomorphism Theorems: The structure of a quotient of an algebraic object is determined by the kernel of a homomorphism.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_4.english: The Fundamental Theorem of Galois Theory
01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_4.text: The Fundamental Theorem of Galois Theory: The symmetries (automorphisms) of a field extension perfectly mirror the structure of its intermediate fields.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_4.text: Representation Theory: Groups can be studied by how they act as linear transformations on vector spaces.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_5.text: The Tensor Product: The tensor product is the "right" way to multiply vector spaces.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_5.text: Classification of Finite Abelian Groups: Every finite abelian group can be uniquely decomposed into a product of cyclic groups of prime-power order.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_6.text: Cayley's Theorem: Every abstract group can be realized as a group of permutations.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_7.text: Sylow Theorems: Every finite group contains subgroups of the largest possible prime-power order, and these subgroups are all "essentially the same" (conjugate).

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_8.text: Burnside's Lemma (Cauchy-Frobenius Lemma): To count the number of truly distinct objects (up to symmetry), average the number of objects fixed by each symmetry operation.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_9.text: Jordan-Hölder Theorem: Every finite group can be broken down into simple groups (groups with no proper normal subgroups), and this decomposition is essentially unique — the "prime factorization" of groups.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_10.text: Wedderburn's Little Theorem: In finite algebra, you get commutativity of multiplication "for free" — there are no noncommutative finite division rings.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_11.text: Artin-Wedderburn Theorem: Semisimple rings — the "nicely decomposable" rings — are completely classified: they are products of matrix rings over division rings.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_12.text: Hilbert's Basis Theorem: If a ring has the property that every ideal can be described by finitely many generators, then so does its polynomial ring.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_13.text: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz: Over an algebraically closed field, a system of polynomial equations either has a solution or the polynomials generate the entire ring.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_14.text: Maschke's Theorem: For finite groups over "good" fields (characteristic zero or coprime to the group order), every representation breaks cleanly into irreducible building blocks.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_15.text: Schur's Lemma: If two irreducible representations are connected by a map that respects the group action, the map must be either zero or an isomorphism -- there is no middle ground.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_16.text: Nakayama's Lemma: Nakayama's lemma says that for finitely generated modules over local or semi-local rings, if an ideal in the Jacobson radical "absorbs" the module (IM = M), then the module must be zero.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_7.text: Homological Algebra (Ext and Tor): Homological algebra studies the "obstructions" that arise when algebraic operations (like taking homomorphisms or tensor products) fail to preserve exact sequences.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_17.english: The Classification of Finite Simple Groups
01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_17.text: The Classification of Finite Simple Groups: Every finite group can be built from simple groups (the Jordan-Holder theorem), and the classification identifies all possible simple building blocks.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_18.text: The Snake Lemma: The snake lemma is the fundamental tool for building long exact sequences in algebra.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_19.text: The Quillen-Suslin Theorem (Serre's Conjecture): Over a polynomial ring in several variables, every "locally free" module (one that looks free in a neighborhood of each point) is actually globally free -- it has a basis.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_8.text: Morita Equivalence: Two rings are Morita equivalent if their module theories are indistinguishable -- every construction and theorem about modules over one ring translates perfectly to the other.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_9.text: Tropical Geometry Foundations: Tropical geometry converts curved algebraic geometry into straight-line, piecewise-linear geometry.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_10.text: Operads and Higher Algebraic Structures: Operads are a mathematical framework for describing algebraic operations with multiple inputs and one output, and the laws they satisfy.

01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_20.english: The Langlands Correspondence for GL(n) over Function Fields
01_mathematics_algebra_theorem_20.text: The Langlands Correspondence for GL(n) over Function Fields: The Langlands correspondence is one of the deepest organizing principles in modern mathematics.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_11.text: Derived Algebraic Geometry: Derived algebraic geometry upgrades classical geometry by keeping track of all the "hidden" algebraic information that is normally lost.

01_mathematics_algebra_principle_12.text: Infinity-Operads and Higher Algebra (Lurie): p-adic Hodge theory is the p-adic analogue of the deep connection between topology and calculus that Hodge theory provides over the complex numbers.

01_mathematics_analysis_axiom_1_2.text: Total Order Axioms: The real numbers are lined up in order, and this order is compatible with arithmetic.

01_mathematics_analysis_axiom_1_3.english: Completeness (Least Upper Bound Property) — THE KEY AXIOM
01_mathematics_analysis_axiom_1_3.text: Completeness (Least Upper Bound Property) — THE KEY AXIOM: There are no "gaps" in the real number line.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_1.text: The ε-δ Definition of Limit: f(x) approaches L as x approaches a if we can make f(x) as close to L as we want by making x sufficiently close to a.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_2.text: Continuity: A function is continuous if small changes in input produce small changes in output.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_3.text: The Derivative (Definition of Differentiation): The derivative is the instantaneous rate of change — the slope of the tangent line at a point.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_4.text: The Riemann Integral: The integral is the "area under the curve," computed as the limit of increasingly fine Riemann sums.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_1.text: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: Differentiation and integration are inverse operations.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_5.text: Convergence of Sequences and Series: A sequence converges if its terms get arbitrarily close to a limit value and stay there.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_6.text: Compactness (Heine-Borel Theorem): Compact sets are the "nice" sets where infinite processes behave well — every sequence has a convergent subsequence, and continuous functions achieve their bounds.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_7.text: Metric Space Axioms (Generalized Analysis): A metric space is any set with a notion of "distance" satisfying three natural properties.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_8.text: Measure and Integration (Lebesgue Theory): A measure assigns a "size" (length, area, volume, probability) to sets in a way that is non-negative and additive.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_2.text: Banach Fixed-Point Theorem (Contraction Mapping Principle): If a map always brings points closer together in a complete space, it has exactly one fixed point, and iterating the map from anywhere converges to it.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_3.text: The Implicit Function Theorem: If a system of equations is satisfied at a point and a certain non-degeneracy condition holds, then the equations locally define some variables as smooth functions of the others.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_4.text: Stokes' Theorem (General Form): The integral of a derivative over a region equals the integral of the original form over the boundary.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_9.text: Banach and Hilbert Spaces (Functional Analysis): Functional analysis extends linear algebra and analysis to infinite-dimensional spaces — spaces of functions, sequences, and operators.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_5.text: Taylor's Theorem and Power Series: Any sufficiently smooth function can be approximated by a polynomial.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_6.text: Hahn-Banach Theorem: You can always extend a bounded linear measurement from a part of a space to the entire space without increasing its size.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_7.text: Open Mapping Theorem (Banach-Schauder): A surjective bounded linear map between complete spaces automatically maps open sets to open sets.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_8.text: Closed Graph Theorem: For a linear operator between complete spaces, you can verify continuity by checking a seemingly weaker condition: that the graph is a closed set.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_9.text: Riesz Representation Theorem: In a Hilbert space, every continuous linear measurement is secretly an inner product with some fixed element.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_10.text: Stone-Weierstrass Theorem: On a compact space, any "sufficiently rich" collection of continuous functions can approximate any continuous function as closely as desired.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_11.text: Spectral Theorem for Self-Adjoint Operators: Every self-adjoint operator can be "diagonalized" using a spectral measure.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_12.text: Fubini's Theorem: When integrating a function of two variables, you can compute the double integral by integrating one variable at a time, in either order.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_13.text: Dominated Convergence Theorem: If a sequence of functions converges pointwise and is uniformly bounded by an integrable function, then the limit of the integrals equals the integral of the limit.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_14.text: Arzela-Ascoli Theorem: A family of continuous functions on a compact space has a uniformly convergent subsequence if and only if the functions are uniformly bounded and "equally continuous" (equicontinuous).

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_15.text: Uniform Boundedness Principle (Banach-Steinhaus): If a family of linear operators is bounded at every individual point, then there is a single bound that works for all points and all operators simultaneously.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_16.text: The Sobolev Embedding Theorem: The Sobolev embedding theorem tells you that if a function has enough weak derivatives in L^p, it is automatically smoother than you might expect.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_17.text: The Lax-Milgram Theorem: The Lax-Milgram theorem guarantees that a large class of variational problems have unique solutions.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_10.text: Distribution Theory (Schwartz): Distribution theory extends calculus to "functions" that are too singular to be ordinary functions, like the Dirac delta "function" (zero everywhere except at one point, yet integrating to 1).

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_18.text: Riesz-Thorin Interpolation Theorem: If a linear operator works well on two different L^p spaces, it automatically works on all the intermediate spaces in between, with a bound that interpolates smoothly between the two extremes.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_19.text: The Baire Category Theorem: In a complete space, "most" points (in the topological sense) avoid any countable collection of "thin" (nowhere-dense) sets.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_20.english: Carleson's Theorem (Pointwise Convergence of Fourier Series)
01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_20.text: Carleson's Theorem (Pointwise Convergence of Fourier Series): If you decompose a square-integrable function into its Fourier series (sines and cosines), the partial sums of the series actually converge back to the original function at almost every point.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_11.text: Ultraproducts and Nonstandard Analysis: Nonstandard analysis provides a rigorous framework for infinitesimals -- infinitely small quantities that Leibniz and Euler used freely but that were banished by the epsilon-delta revolution.

01_mathematics_analysis_principle_12.english: Optimal Transport and the Wasserstein Distance
01_mathematics_analysis_principle_12.text: Optimal Transport and the Wasserstein Distance: Optimal transport theory solves the problem of moving one distribution of mass to another at minimum cost.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_22.text: The Regularity Structures Framework (Hairer): Regularity structures provide a way to make mathematical sense of equations involving products of extremely rough (distributional) objects -- operations that are classically meaningless.

01_mathematics_analysis_theorem_23.text: Random Matrix Universality (Wigner-Dyson-Mehta): Schramm-Loewner evolution is a family of random curves that describes the interfaces in two-dimensional critical phenomena — the boundaries between phases in systems like percolation, the Ising model, and random walks at their critical points.

01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.english: The Differential Equation as the Language of Change
01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.text: The Differential Equation as the Language of Change: Most laws of nature and engineering are expressed as relationships between quantities and how they change.

01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_theor.text: Existence and Uniqueness (Picard-Lindelöf Theorem): If the rate of change depends smoothly on the current state, then the future is uniquely determined by the present.

01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.english: Linear Algebra as the Framework for Linear Systems
01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.text: Linear Algebra as the Framework for Linear Systems: Linear algebra is the mathematics of systems of equations, transformations, and vector spaces.

01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.english: The Calculus of Variations and the Principle of Least Action
01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.text: The Calculus of Variations and the Principle of Least Action: Nature "chooses" the path that extremizes (usually minimizes) a certain quantity — the action.
Fourier Analysis — Decomposition into Frequencies: Any signal can be broken down into a sum of pure sine and cosine waves at different frequencies.
Optimization — Finding the Best Solution: Optimization finds the best solution among all feasible options.
Numerical Approximation and Error Analysis: Since computers work with finite numbers, we must approximate continuous mathematics.
Dimensional Analysis and Scaling: Physical laws must be dimensionally consistent.
Dynamical Systems and Chaos Theory: Dynamical systems theory studies how systems evolve over time.
Perturbation Theory and Asymptotic Analysis: When a problem is "almost" a simpler problem, solve the simple version first and then correct for the small complication step by step.
Integral Transforms (Laplace, Fourier, Z-Transform): Integral transforms convert hard problems (differential equations) into easier ones (algebraic equations) by changing the domain of the function.
Green's Functions and Fundamental Solutions: A Green's function is the system's response to a point impulse.

01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.english: Monte Carlo Methods and Stochastic Simulation
01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.text: Monte Carlo Methods and Stochastic Simulation: You can estimate complicated quantities (integrals, probabilities, expectations) by generating random samples and averaging.
Finite Element Method: The finite element method breaks a complicated domain into small simple pieces, solves the problem approximately on each piece, and assembles the results.

01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_theor.text: Noether's Theorem (Symmetry and Conservation Laws): Symmetries in the laws of physics directly produce conservation laws.

01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.text: Optimal Control Theory (Pontryagin's Maximum Principle): Optimal control theory finds the best way to steer a dynamical system over time.
Wavelet Theory: Wavelets are "little waves" that can decompose signals into components that are localized in both time and frequency simultaneously.
Information Theory (Shannon): Information theory quantifies information, communication limits, and data compression.
Lyapunov Stability Theory: Lyapunov stability theory provides a method to prove stability of an equilibrium without solving the differential equation.
Bifurcation Theory: Bifurcation theory studies how the qualitative behavior of a dynamical system changes as you tune a parameter.
Homogenization Theory: Many physical materials have fine-scale structure (composites, porous media, polycrystals).
Spectral Methods for PDEs: Spectral methods solve differential equations by representing the solution as a sum of smooth basis functions (like Fourier modes or Chebyshev polynomials).

Compressed Sensing (Candes-Tao-Donoho): Compressed sensing says that sparse signals -- those with few nonzero components relative to their dimension -- can be perfectly reconstructed from far fewer measurements than traditional sampling theory (Nyquist-Shannon) requires.
Tensor Network Methods: High-dimensional problems suffer from the "curse of dimensionality" -- the number of parameters grows exponentially with dimension.
Randomized Numerical Linear Algebra: Classical matrix algorithms (SVD, eigendecomposition, least squares) are too slow for modern massive datasets.

Model Order Reduction (Reduced Basis Methods): Many engineering problems require solving the same type of equation millions of times for different parameter values (e.g., optimizing an airfoil shape, or real-time control of a thermal system).
Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs): Physics-informed neural networks embed known physical laws (differential equations) directly into the training process of a neural network.
Data-Driven Discovery of Governing Equations (SINDy): SINDy automatically discovers the mathematical equations governing a dynamical system from measured data.

fp_01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_pr.english: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and Scientific Machine Learning
fp_01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_pr.text: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and Scientific Machine Learning: Physics-informed neural networks encode the laws of physics directly into the training of a neural network.

01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.english: Wasserstein Gradient Flows and Optimal Transport in Applied Mathematics
01_mathematics_applied_mathematics_princ.text: Wasserstein Gradient Flows and Optimal Transport in Applied Mathematics: Many evolution equations in physics can be understood as "rolling downhill" on an energy landscape, where the "distance" is the optimal transport distance.
Topological Data Analysis in Applications: Persistent homology extracts the "shape" of data at all scales simultaneously.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_1.text: The Axiomatic Method: Mathematics works by choosing starting assumptions and rigorously deriving everything else from them.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_2.text: Consistency as a Necessary Condition: A mathematical system must not contradict itself.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_3.text: Logicism — Mathematics Reduces to Logic: Mathematics is just very elaborate logic.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_4.text: Formalism — Mathematics as Symbol Manipulation: Mathematics is a game played with symbols according to rules.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_5.text: Intuitionism — Mathematics as Mental Construction: Mathematics is a human activity of building mental constructions.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_6.text: The Principle of Mathematical Induction: If something is true for zero, and being true for any number means it's true for the next number, then it's true for all natural numbers.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_7.text: The Peano Axioms (Foundations of Arithmetic): The natural numbers are built from zero by repeatedly taking successors, the successor function is one-to-one, zero is the starting point, and you can prove things about all numbers using induction.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_8.text: The Concept of Isomorphism (Structural Identity): Mathematics doesn't care about *what* things are, only about *how they relate to each other*.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_9.text: Definition by Recursion / Well-Founded Recursion: You can define mathematical objects by specifying a starting point and a rule for building each piece from previously built pieces.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_10.text: Completeness and Categoricity: A complete theory settles every question.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_11.english: The Abstraction Principle and Foundational Crisis
01_mathematics_foundations_principle_11.text: The Abstraction Principle and Foundational Crisis: The idea that "every definable collection is a set" leads to paradox.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_12.english: Constructive Mathematics and the BHK Interpretation
01_mathematics_foundations_principle_12.text: Constructive Mathematics and the BHK Interpretation: In constructive mathematics, proving something exists means actually producing it.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_13.text: The Well-Ordering Principle (General): A set is well-ordered if no matter what subset you pick, there is always a smallest element.

01_mathematics_foundations_theorem_13.text: Gödel's Completeness Theorem (for Foundations): First-order logic is perfectly calibrated: anything that must be true (in every possible interpretation) can be formally proved, and anything provable is indeed true.

01_mathematics_foundations_theorem_14.text: The Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem (for Foundations): First-order logic cannot control the size of infinite structures.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_15.text: The Curry-Howard Correspondence: Proofs and programs are secretly the same thing.

01_mathematics_foundations_principle_16.text: Structuralism in Mathematics: Chromatic homotopy theory reveals that the stable homotopy groups of spheres — one of the most fundamental and mysterious objects in all of mathematics — are organized into layers of increasing complexity, like a prism decomposing white light into its component colors.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pos.text: Two Points Determine a Line: You can always draw a straight line between two points.
Line Extension: A straight line goes on forever in both directions.
Circle Construction: You can always draw a circle of any size around any point.
Right Angle Universality: A right angle is the same everywhere — it is a universal standard of perpendicularity.
The Parallel Postulate: Parallel lines never meet (in flat space).

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_axi.text: Finite Intersections: A topology defines which subsets are "open" — this determines the notions of continuity, convergence, and connectedness.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.text: Homeomorphism (Topological Equivalence): Two shapes are "the same" topologically if one can be continuously deformed into the other without tearing or gluing.
Euler Characteristic: The alternating sum of vertices, edges, and faces of a polyhedron is always 2.
The Fundamental Group: The fundamental group measures the "holes" in a space by studying loops that cannot be shrunk to a point.
Riemannian Metric (Differential Geometry): A Riemannian metric is a way of measuring distance on a curved surface (or higher-dimensional space) that can vary from point to point.
Compactness in Topology: A compact space is one where you can always extract a finite amount of information to describe its covering.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_the.text: Classification of Closed Surfaces: There is a complete list of all possible 2D surfaces — they are classified by their number of "handles" (genus) and whether they are orientable.
The Gauss-Bonnet Theorem: The total curvature of a closed surface is completely determined by its topology (the Euler characteristic).

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.english: Covering Spaces and the Fundamental Group
01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.text: Covering Spaces and the Fundamental Group: Covering spaces "unwrap" a topological space.
Homology and Cohomology: Homology counts the different types of holes in a space: H₀ counts connected components, H₁ counts loops, H₂ counts enclosed cavities, and so on.
Smooth Manifolds and Differential Forms: Smooth manifolds are spaces that locally look like ℝⁿ and support calculus.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_the.text: Brouwer Fixed-Point Theorem: If you continuously stir a cup of coffee, at least one point ends up where it started.
Poincaré Duality: On a closed oriented manifold, k-dimensional holes are perfectly mirrored by (n-k)-dimensional holes.
The de Rham Theorem: There are two seemingly different ways to detect holes in a manifold: using differential forms (calculus) or using singular chains (algebra).
The Atiyah-Singer Index Theorem: The number of solutions of a differential equation on a manifold is determined by the topology of the manifold.
The Riemann-Roch Theorem: On an algebraic curve, the number of independent meromorphic functions with prescribed poles and zeros is determined by the degree of the divisor and the genus of the curve.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.text: Fiber Bundles and Connections: A fiber bundle attaches a copy of a "fiber" space to each point of a "base" space in a smoothly varying way.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_the.text: Thurston's Geometrization Conjecture (Perelman's Theorem): Every compact 3-dimensional space can be decomposed into pieces, each of which has one of exactly eight possible geometric structures.
Nash Embedding Theorem: Any curved space, no matter how abstract, can be realized as a surface sitting inside a high-dimensional flat space without distorting any distances.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.text: Morse Theory: The topology of a manifold is encoded in the critical points of any "generic" smooth function on it.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_the.text: The h-Cobordism Theorem: In dimensions 6 and above, if two manifolds are connected by a "cylinder" that is topologically trivial (homotopically a product), then they are actually the same smooth manifold.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.text: Symplectic Geometry: Symplectic geometry studies spaces with a special structure that generalizes the phase space of classical mechanics.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_the.text: The Lefschetz Fixed-Point Theorem: The Lefschetz theorem provides an algebraic test for fixed points: compute an alternating sum of traces of the map's action on homology groups.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.text: Characteristic Classes (Chern, Pontryagin, Stiefel-Whitney): Characteristic classes are topological invariants that detect whether a vector bundle is "twisted." A trivial bundle (a product) has all characteristic classes zero, but a nontrivial bundle (like the Mobius band as a line bundle over the circle) has nonzero classes.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_the.text: Seiberg-Witten Invariants and 4-Manifold Topology: Seiberg-Witten invariants are numbers computed from solutions of differential equations on 4-dimensional manifolds that detect subtle differences in smooth structure invisible to topology alone.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.english: Persistent Homology and Topological Data Analysis
01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.text: Persistent Homology and Topological Data Analysis: Persistent homology extracts the "shape" of data at all scales simultaneously.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_the.text: The Uniformization Theorem: There are only three "shapes" for simply connected surfaces with a conformal structure: the sphere, the plane, and the hyperbolic plane.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.text: Cobordism Theory: Two manifolds are "cobordant" if they together form the boundary of a higher-dimensional manifold.

fp_01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_.text: Tropical Geometry and Polyhedral Topology: Tropical geometry replaces smooth curves and surfaces with networks of straight-line segments and flat pieces.
The Cobordism Hypothesis (Lurie): The cobordism hypothesis says that a topological quantum field theory is entirely determined by what it assigns to a single point.

01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.english: Motivic Cohomology and the Bloch-Kato Conjectures
01_mathematics_geometry_and_topology_pri.text: Motivic Cohomology and the Bloch-Kato Conjectures: Percolation theory studies when random networks become connected.

01_mathematics_logic_and_set_theory_axio.text: Law of Identity: A thing is what it is.
Law of Non-Contradiction: Nothing can both be and not be at the same time in the same respect.
Law of Excluded Middle: For any statement, either it or its negation holds.
Modus Ponens (Rule of Detachment): If something is true, and that something implies a second thing, then the second thing is also true.
Universal Instantiation: If something is true for everything, it is true for any particular thing.
Existential Generalization: If something is true of a particular thing, then there exists something for which it is true.
Axiom of Extensionality: Two sets are equal if and only if they have exactly the same members.
Axiom of Empty Set: The empty set exists.
Axiom of Pairing: For any two objects, there exists a set containing exactly those two objects.
Axiom of Union: For any collection of sets, there exists a set that contains all elements that belong to at least one set in the collection.
Axiom of Power Set: For any set A, there exists a set containing all subsets of A.
Axiom Schema of Separation (Aussonderung): Given any set and any property, there exists a subset of that set containing exactly those elements satisfying the property.
Axiom Schema of Replacement: The image of a set under any definable function is also a set.
Axiom of Infinity: There exists at least one infinite set — specifically, a set that contains ∅ and, for each of its elements x, also contains x ∪ {x}.
Axiom of Foundation (Regularity): Every non-empty set has a member that shares no elements with the set.
Axiom of Choice (AC): Given any collection of non-empty boxes, you can always choose one item from each box — even if the collection is infinite and there is no rule for making the choice.

01_mathematics_logic_and_set_theory_theo.text: Gödel's First Incompleteness Theorem: No sufficiently powerful consistent system can prove all true statements about natural numbers.
Gödel's Second Incompleteness Theorem: A mathematical system cannot use its own axioms to prove that it is free of contradictions.
Gödel's Completeness Theorem (for First-Order Logic): First-order logic is "complete" — anything that is true in all possible interpretations can be formally proved.
Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem: First-order logic cannot "pin down" the size of infinite structures.

01_mathematics_logic_and_set_theory_axio.text: Martin's Axiom (MA): Martin's Axiom says that certain "generic" objects can be found as long as you don't ask for too many conditions simultaneously (fewer than continuum-many).

01_mathematics_logic_and_set_theory_theo.english: The Compactness Theorem (for Logic & Set Theory)
01_mathematics_logic_and_set_theory_theo.text: The Compactness Theorem (for Logic & Set Theory): You cannot create an inconsistency using infinitely many axioms unless some finite subset is already inconsistent.

01_mathematics_logic_and_set_theory_prin.text: Ramsey Theory: Complete disorder is impossible.
Large Cardinal Axioms (Overview): Large cardinals are "very large infinities" whose existence cannot be proved from the standard axioms but can be assumed as extra axioms.

01_mathematics_logic_and_set_theory_theo.text: Borel Determinacy: Absoluteness means a statement's truth value does not change between different models of set theory.

01_mathematics_logic_and_set_theory_theo.english: Cohen's Independence of the Continuum Hypothesis
01_mathematics_logic_and_set_theory_theo.text: Cohen's Independence of the Continuum Hypothesis: The effective topos is a mathematical universe in which every function is computable, every set is constructively defined, and the classical law of excluded middle fails.

01_mathematics_number_theory_axiom_1.english: The Peano Axioms Define the Natural Numbers
01_mathematics_number_theory_axiom_1.text: The Peano Axioms Define the Natural Numbers: The natural numbers are built from zero by counting: 0, 1, 2, 3, ...

01_mathematics_number_theory_principle_1.text: The Well-Ordering Principle: Among any collection of natural numbers, there is always a smallest one.

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_1.text: The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic: Every whole number greater than 1 is either prime or can be built by multiplying primes together, and there is only one way to do it (ignoring order).

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_2.text: Euclid's Theorem (Infinitude of Primes): The list of primes never ends.

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_3.text: The Division Algorithm: Division with remainder always works and gives a unique answer.

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_4.text: The Euclidean Algorithm: To find the greatest common divisor of two numbers, keep dividing and taking remainders until you hit zero.

01_mathematics_number_theory_principle_2.text: Modular Arithmetic (Congruence): Two numbers are "the same mod n" if they have the same remainder when divided by n.

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_5.text: Fermat's Little Theorem: Raise any number to the power (p-1), divide by the prime p, and the remainder is always 1.

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_6.text: Quadratic Reciprocity: Whether p is a square mod q is intimately related to whether q is a square mod p, in a symmetric and beautiful way.

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_7.text: The Prime Number Theorem: The number of primes up to x is approximately x divided by the natural logarithm of x.

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_8.text: The Chinese Remainder Theorem: If you know the remainders when dividing by several coprime numbers, there is exactly one number (up to the product) that gives all those remainders.

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_9.english: Dirichlet's Theorem on Primes in Arithmetic Progressions
01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_9.text: Dirichlet's Theorem on Primes in Arithmetic Progressions: There are infinitely many primes in any arithmetic progression whose first term and common difference share no common factor (e.g., infinitely many primes of the form 4k+1).

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_10.english: Diophantine Equations and Fermat's Last Theorem
01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_10.text: Diophantine Equations and Fermat's Last Theorem: You cannot find whole numbers that satisfy the Pythagorean equation if the exponent is 3 or higher.

01_mathematics_number_theory_principle_3.text: p-adic Numbers: The p-adic numbers are an alternative way of measuring "closeness" — two numbers are close if their difference is divisible by a high power of p.

01_mathematics_number_theory_principle_4.text: The Riemann Hypothesis: The Riemann Hypothesis is the most important unsolved problem in mathematics.

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_11.text: Hasse-Minkowski Theorem (Local-Global Principle): To determine if a quadratic equation has a rational solution, it suffices to check that it has solutions in the real numbers and in all p-adic number systems.

01_mathematics_number_theory_principle_5.text: Class Field Theory: Class field theory completely classifies all abelian extensions (extensions with commutative Galois group) of a number field.

01_mathematics_number_theory_theorem_12.text: Mordell's Conjecture (Faltings' Theorem): The polynomial method is the surprising discovery that polynomials — objects from algebra — can solve problems in combinatorics that seemed to require entirely different tools.

01_mathematics_probability_and_statistic.text: Non-Negativity: Probabilities are never negative.
Normalization: Something must happen — the probability of the entire sample space is 1.
Countable Additivity (σ-Additivity): The probability of one of several mutually exclusive events occurring is the sum of their individual probabilities.
Conditional Probability and Bayes' Theorem: The probability of A given that B occurred is the probability of both happening divided by the probability of B.
Independence: Two events are independent if knowing that one occurred gives no information about whether the other occurred.
Random Variable and Expectation: A random variable assigns a number to each outcome.
Law of Large Numbers (LLN): The average of many independent observations converges to the expected value.
Central Limit Theorem (CLT): The sum (or average) of many independent random variables is approximately normally distributed, regardless of the original distribution.
Likelihood and Maximum Likelihood Estimation: The likelihood measures how "plausible" each parameter value is given the observed data.
The Bayesian Framework: Start with prior beliefs about parameters, observe data, update beliefs using Bayes' theorem to get posterior beliefs.
Sufficiency and the Rao-Blackwell Theorem: A sufficient statistic captures all the information in the data about the parameter.
The Markov Property: A system has the Markov property if knowing the current state tells you everything you need to predict the future — the past history is irrelevant.
Martingale Theory: A martingale is a "fair game" — on average, the next value equals the current value.
The Cramér-Rao Lower Bound: There is a minimum possible variance for any unbiased estimator.
Neyman-Pearson Lemma (Hypothesis Testing): The best test for distinguishing two hypotheses is based on the ratio of how likely the data is under each hypothesis.
Large Deviations Principle (Cramér's Theorem): The probability of a large fluctuation from the average decreases exponentially fast with the number of observations.

Concentration Inequalities: These inequalities bound the probability that a random variable or average deviates far from its expected value — essential for understanding when randomness is "well-behaved."
Fisher Information: Fisher information measures how much information a random observation carries about an unknown parameter.

Rao-Blackwell Theorem: If you have a sufficient statistic (a summary that captures all the information about the parameter), you can improve any estimator by conditioning it on the sufficient statistic.
Exponential Families: Exponential families are a unified class of probability distributions with especially clean mathematical properties.
The Bootstrap Principle: Free probability theory is a version of probability that works for noncommutative objects like random matrices.

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_po.text: The Cosmological Principle: No place in the universe is "special." On large scales, it looks the same from every point and in every direction.

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_la.text: The Friedmann Equations: These equations describe how the universe expands (or contracts).
Hubble's Law (Hubble-Lemaître Law): Distant galaxies are moving away from us, and the farther away they are, the faster they recede.

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.text: Stellar Structure — Hydrostatic Equilibrium: A star is a balance between gravity (pulling inward) and pressure (pushing outward).

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.english: Nuclear Fusion as the Energy Source of Stars
02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.text: Nuclear Fusion as the Energy Source of Stars: Stars shine by fusing light elements into heavier ones.

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.english: The Big Bang and Cosmic Evolution
02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.text: The Big Bang and Cosmic Evolution: The universe started as an incredibly hot, dense point and has been expanding ever since.
Black Holes: When enough mass is compressed into a small enough volume, it collapses into a black hole — an object from which nothing can escape.
Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB): The CMB is the "afterglow" of the Big Bang — ancient light that has been traveling through space for 13.8 billion years.
Olbers' Paradox and Its Resolution: Why is the night sky dark?
Dark Matter: Most of the matter in the universe is invisible — it doesn't emit or absorb light.

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.english: Dark Energy and the Cosmological Constant
02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.text: Dark Energy and the Cosmological Constant: The universe's expansion is speeding up, not slowing down.
Nucleosynthesis (Big Bang and Stellar): The lightest elements (hydrogen, helium, lithium) were forged in the first minutes after the Big Bang.
Jeans Instability: A gas cloud in space will collapse under its own gravity if it is large and massive enough that gravity overwhelms the pressure trying to hold it up.

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_la.text: Chandrasekhar Limit: There is a maximum mass that a white dwarf can have, about 1.4 times the mass of the Sun.

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.text: Eddington Luminosity: There is a maximum brightness a star can have — the Eddington luminosity.

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_la.text: Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff (TOV) Limit: Just as white dwarfs have a maximum mass (Chandrasekhar limit), neutron stars have their own maximum mass — the TOV limit.

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.text: Cosmic Inflation: In the first tiny fraction of a second after the Big Bang, the universe expanded exponentially fast — a region smaller than a proton was inflated to cosmic scales.
The Virial Theorem in Astrophysics: For a star or galaxy cluster in gravitational equilibrium, there is a precise relationship between its kinetic (thermal) energy and its gravitational energy.
The Saha Ionization Equation: The Saha equation tells you what fraction of atoms are ionized at a given temperature and density.

Stellar Structure Equations (Complete Set): Four equations completely determine the structure of a star: how mass is distributed, how pressure balances gravity, how energy flows outward (by radiation or convection), and how nuclear reactions generate energy.
Bondi Accretion (Spherical Accretion): When a massive object (like a black hole or star) sits in a sea of gas, it gravitationally attracts the surrounding gas and accretes it.

Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) — Frozen-In Flux: In a hot, electrically conducting gas (plasma), the magnetic field is trapped in the fluid and moves with it — the field lines are "frozen in." This coupling between the magnetic field and the fluid motion creates a rich set of phenomena: magnetic pressure, magnetic tension, and waves that travel along field lines.
The Epoch of Reionization: After the Big Bang cooled enough for atoms to form, the universe went dark for hundreds of millions of years.

02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.english: The Tully-Fisher Relation and Distance Indicators
02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology_pr.text: The Tully-Fisher Relation and Distance Indicators: Brighter spiral galaxies rotate faster.
Baryon Acoustic Oscillations (BAO): Sound waves traveled through the early universe (when it was a hot plasma of photons and baryons) just like sound waves through air.

Gravitational Wave Standard Sirens: Gravitational waves from merging compact objects (neutron stars, black holes) encode their distance directly in the signal strength -- the waveform tells you both the intrinsic "brightness" and the observed amplitude, giving the distance without needing any distance ladder.
Kilonova Physics and r-Process Nucleosynthesis: When two neutron stars collide, they fling out extremely neutron-rich material at a significant fraction of the speed of light.
21-cm Cosmology: Neutral hydrogen atoms emit or absorb radio waves at a specific wavelength (21 cm) when the electron's spin flips.

Multi-Messenger Astronomy: Just as human senses work better together (seeing and hearing an event gives more information than either alone), astronomy becomes far more powerful when combining different types of signals from the same cosmic event.

fp_02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology.english: The Hubble Tension and Precision Cosmology
fp_02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology.text: The Hubble Tension and Precision Cosmology: Two fundamentally different ways of measuring the expansion rate of the universe give incompatible answers.

fp_02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology.english: Fast Radio Bursts and Cosmological Probes
fp_02_physics_astrophysics_and_cosmology.text: Fast Radio Bursts and Cosmological Probes: Multi-messenger astronomy is the practice of observing the universe using fundamentally different "messengers" — gravitational waves, light, neutrinos, and cosmic rays — simultaneously.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_law_1.text: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia): Objects don't change their motion unless something pushes or pulls them.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_law_2.text: Newton's Second Law (F = ma): Force equals mass times acceleration.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_law_3.text: Newton's Third Law (Action-Reaction): Every action has an equal and opposite reaction.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_law_4.text: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation: Every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_principle.text: Conservation of Energy: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed from one form to another.
Conservation of Momentum: In an isolated system, the total momentum before an event equals the total momentum after.
Conservation of Angular Momentum: Spinning objects keep spinning at the same rate unless twisted by an external torque.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_principle.english: The Principle of Least Action (Lagrangian Mechanics)
02_physics_classical_mechanics_principle.text: The Principle of Least Action (Lagrangian Mechanics): Nature chooses the path that makes the action stationary.
Hamiltonian Mechanics and Phase Space: Rewrite mechanics in terms of position and momentum (not velocity).
Routhian Mechanics: Routhian mechanics is a hybrid between Lagrangian and Hamiltonian approaches.

Symplectic Integrators: When simulating Hamiltonian systems numerically, standard methods (like Runge-Kutta) violate the geometric structure of the phase space, causing energy to drift over long simulations.
D'Alembert's Principle: D'Alembert's principle transforms a dynamics problem into a statics problem by treating inertial forces as negative applied forces.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_theorem_2.text: Liouville's Theorem: If you follow a swarm of phase space points evolving under Hamilton's equations, the volume they occupy never changes — the "fluid" of phase space is incompressible.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_law_5.text: Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion: Planetary orbits are ellipses, planets move faster when closer to the Sun, and more distant planets take longer to orbit.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_theorem_3.text: The Virial Theorem: For bound gravitational systems, the average kinetic energy is half the magnitude of the average potential energy.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_theorem_4.text: The KAM Theorem (Kolmogorov-Arnold-Moser): For nearly integrable systems, most of the orderly behavior survives small perturbations.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_principle.english: Maupertuis' Principle (Principle of Least Action — Original Form)
02_physics_classical_mechanics_principle.text: Maupertuis' Principle (Principle of Least Action — Original Form): Maupertuis' principle is the earliest variational principle in physics: nature chooses the path that minimizes (or makes stationary) the "effort" of motion at fixed energy.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_theorem_5.text: Bertrand's Theorem: If you want a particle orbiting under a central force to always return to its starting point (a closed orbit), there are exactly two force laws that guarantee this: the inverse-square law (gravity, electrostatics) and the harmonic oscillator (springs).

02_physics_classical_mechanics_principle.text: Hamilton-Jacobi Theory: Hamilton-Jacobi theory reformulates mechanics as a problem of finding wavefronts in configuration space.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_theorem_6.text: Poincaré Recurrence Theorem: In a bounded system governed by Hamilton's equations, the system will eventually return to a state arbitrarily close to where it started.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_principle.text: Adiabatic Invariants: When you slowly change the conditions of a periodic system, a special combination of energy and frequency stays constant.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_theorem_7.text: Arnold Diffusion: The KAM theorem guarantees that most regular orbits survive small perturbations, but in systems with three or more degrees of freedom, the remaining chaotic regions are connected.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_principle.text: Canonical Perturbation Theory: Canonical perturbation theory provides a systematic way to approximate the behavior of a nearly-integrable Hamiltonian system.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_theorem_8.text: Noether's Second Theorem: While Noether's first theorem connects global symmetries to conservation laws, her second theorem addresses local (gauge) symmetries — those parameterized by arbitrary functions.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_principle.text: Nambu Mechanics (Generalized Hamiltonian Systems): Standard Hamiltonian mechanics uses one energy function and the Poisson bracket (a two-input operation) to generate time evolution.
Contact Geometry in Mechanics (Dissipative Systems): Symplectic geometry describes energy-conserving systems perfectly, but real mechanical systems lose energy to friction, damping, and dissipation.

Port-Hamiltonian Systems: Port-Hamiltonian systems provide a unified framework for modeling physical systems as energy-storing, energy-dissipating, and energy-exchanging components connected through "ports." Any complex system -- mechanical, electrical, hydraulic, thermal -- can be modeled by connecting port-Hamiltonian subsystems.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_theorem_9.text: The Kolmogorov-Arnold Representation Theorem: The Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem says that any continuous function of several variables, no matter how complicated, can be written as sums and compositions of continuous functions of just one variable.

02_physics_classical_mechanics_principle.text: Geometric Mechanics and Reduction Theory: Geometric mechanics reveals that the symmetries of a mechanical system determine its mathematical structure.

Non-Smooth Mechanics and Contact Dynamics: The Casimir effect demonstrates that empty space is not truly empty — quantum mechanics requires that even a perfect vacuum contains fluctuating electromagnetic fields.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_1.text: The Band Theory of Solids: Electrons in a crystal don't have arbitrary energies — they live in "bands" of allowed energies.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_2.english: Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking and Order Parameters
02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_2.text: Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking and Order Parameters: When a system cools below a critical temperature, it can spontaneously "choose" a preferred direction or configuration, breaking the symmetry of the underlying laws.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_3.text: Phase Transitions and Universality: Near a phase transition, all systems with the same basic symmetry behave the same way, regardless of their microscopic details.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_4.text: Superconductivity (BCS Theory): At very low temperatures, electrons pair up and move through a material without any resistance.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_5.text: Topological Phases of Matter: Some properties of materials are determined by the "shape" of their quantum states in an abstract mathematical sense.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_6.text: The Fermi Liquid Theory: Even though electrons in metals interact strongly with each other, they often behave as if they are nearly free particles with modified properties.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_7.text: Bose-Einstein Condensation: At extremely low temperatures, a collection of bosonic particles all "collapses" into the same quantum state, forming a single giant quantum object visible to the naked eye.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_8.text: Ginzburg-Landau Theory of Phase Transitions: Near a phase transition, you can describe the system using a single "order parameter" (like magnetization in a magnet) and write down a simple energy expression.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_9.text: Anderson Localization: Disorder can trap electrons — not by barriers, but by quantum interference.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_10.english: The Renormalization Group (RG) in Condensed Matter
02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_10.text: The Renormalization Group (RG) in Condensed Matter: To understand large-scale behavior, you can systematically "zoom out" — averaging over small-scale details.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_11.english: The Hubbard Model and Mott Insulators
02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_11.text: The Hubbard Model and Mott Insulators: When electrons repel each other strongly enough, they refuse to move even though band theory says they should be metallic.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_12.text: The Kondo Effect: A single magnetic atom in a metal creates a cloud of electrons around it that screens its magnetism at low temperatures, producing unusual resistance behavior.

02_physics_condensed_matter_theorem_1.text: Mermin-Wagner Theorem: In one and two dimensions, thermal fluctuations are strong enough to destroy any ordered state that breaks a continuous symmetry.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_13.text: The Kosterlitz-Thouless Transition: The KT transition is a phase transition with no conventional order parameter.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_14.text: Berry Phase (Geometric Phase): When you slowly change the parameters of a quantum system and bring them back to where they started, the quantum state remembers the path — it picks up a phase that depends only on the geometry of the path, not on how fast you went.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_15.text: Laughlin's Argument (Fractional Quantum Hall Effect): In a very strong magnetic field at very low temperatures, electrons in two dimensions form an exotic quantum fluid with remarkable properties: the Hall conductance is quantized at rational fractions of e²/h, and the excitations carry fractional electric charge and exhibit statistics that are neither bosonic nor fermionic.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_16.text: Spin-Orbit Coupling: An electron's spin and its motion are coupled: the spin "feels" its motion through the electric field it moves in.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_17.english: Density Functional Theory (DFT) and the Kohn-Sham Equations
02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_17.text: Density Functional Theory (DFT) and the Kohn-Sham Equations: DFT reduces the impossibly complex many-electron problem (solving a wavefunction in 3N dimensions) to solving for the electron density (a function of just 3 variables).

02_physics_condensed_matter_theorem_2.english: Topological Insulators and the Z₂ Invariant
02_physics_condensed_matter_theorem_2.text: Topological Insulators and the Z₂ Invariant: A topological insulator is insulating in its interior but conducts electricity on its surface through special states that are immune to impurities and defects.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_18.english: Green's Function Methods in Many-Body Physics
02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_18.text: Green's Function Methods in Many-Body Physics: The Green's function describes how a particle added to a many-body system propagates through it — absorbing the effects of all the interactions along the way.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_19.text: The Quantum Spin Hall Effect: The quantum spin Hall effect creates a pair of conducting channels at the edges of a 2D material: electrons with spin-up flow one way, electrons with spin-down flow the other way.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_20.text: Bosonization and the Tomonaga-Luttinger Liquid: In one dimension, the familiar picture of electrons behaving as independent quasiparticles (Fermi liquid) completely fails.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_21.english: Weyl Semimetals and Topological Semimetal Phases
02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_21.text: Weyl Semimetals and Topological Semimetal Phases: Weyl semimetals are materials where electrons behave as if they are massless Weyl fermions -- particles first predicted in 1929 but never observed as fundamental particles.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_22.text: Non-Hermitian Physics and Exceptional Points: Standard quantum mechanics requires Hamiltonians to be Hermitian (self-adjoint), guaranteeing real energies and unitary time evolution.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_23.text: Time Crystals (Discrete and Floquet): Crystals break spatial translation symmetry: atoms arrange in a repeating pattern in space.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_24.english: Moire Physics and Flat Bands in Twisted Bilayer Graphene
02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_24.text: Moire Physics and Flat Bands in Twisted Bilayer Graphene: When two sheets of graphene are stacked with a tiny twist (~1.1 degrees), something remarkable happens: the electrons slow down dramatically, forming "flat bands" where kinetic energy nearly vanishes.

02_physics_condensed_matter_principle_25.text: Topological Superconductivity and Majorana Fermions: Topological superconductors host Majorana fermions at their edges -- exotic quasiparticles that are their own antiparticles.

fp_02_physics_condensed_matter_principle.english: Moire Physics and Flat Band Systems
fp_02_physics_condensed_matter_principle.text: Moire Physics and Flat Band Systems: When two sheets of graphene are twisted by about 1.1 degrees relative to each other, the electrons slow down dramatically -- their effective mass diverges and their energy bands become nearly flat.

Non-Hermitian Topology in Condensed Matter: Non-Hermitian topology extends the classification of topological phases to systems that are not energy-conserving -- where particles can be created, destroyed, or have finite lifetimes.
Quantum Spin Liquids: Non-Hermitian physics describes systems that exchange energy with their environment — systems with amplification, absorption, or one-way coupling.

02_physics_electromagnetism_law_1.text: Coulomb's Law: Electric currents and changing electric fields create magnetic fields.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_1.text: The Lorentz Force Law: The force on a charged particle is due to both the electric field (pushes along E) and the magnetic field (pushes perpendicular to velocity and B).

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_2.text: Conservation of Charge: Electric charge is neither created nor destroyed.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_3.text: Electromagnetic Waves: Oscillating electric and magnetic fields propagate through space as waves at the speed of light.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_4.text: Gauge Invariance: The potentials that generate the electromagnetic fields are not unique — different potentials can describe the same physics.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_5.english: Poynting's Theorem (Energy Conservation in EM Fields)
02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_5.text: Poynting's Theorem (Energy Conservation in EM Fields): Electromagnetic energy is conserved: the rate of change of energy in a region plus the energy flowing out equals the work done on charges.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_6.text: Electromagnetic Boundary Conditions: When electromagnetic fields cross a boundary between different materials, certain components must be continuous and others can jump.

02_physics_electromagnetism_law_2.text: Lenz's Law: Nature resists changes in magnetic flux — induced currents always flow in the direction that tries to maintain the original flux.

02_physics_electromagnetism_law_3.text: Biot-Savart Law: A small piece of current-carrying wire produces a magnetic field that circulates around it, inversely proportional to the square of the distance.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_7.english: Larmor Formula (Electromagnetic Radiation from Accelerating Charges)
02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_7.text: Larmor Formula (Electromagnetic Radiation from Accelerating Charges): Accelerating electric charges radiate electromagnetic waves.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_8.text: The Skin Effect: Alternating current does not penetrate deeply into a conductor.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_9.english: Maxwell Stress Tensor (Electromagnetic Momentum and Forces)
02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_9.text: Maxwell Stress Tensor (Electromagnetic Momentum and Forces): Electromagnetic fields carry momentum and can exert forces on objects.

02_physics_electromagnetism_law_4.text: Electromagnetic Induction — Faraday's Law (Integral Form): A changing magnetic flux through a circuit generates a voltage (EMF) that drives current.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_10.english: Jefimenko's Equations (Time-Dependent Generalizations of Coulomb and Biot-Savart)
02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_10.text: Jefimenko's Equations (Time-Dependent Generalizations of Coulomb and Biot-Savart): Jefimenko's equations give the exact electric and magnetic fields produced by any moving charge and current distribution, properly accounting for the finite speed of light.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_11.english: Gauge Fixing — Lorenz and Coulomb Gauges
02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_11.text: Gauge Fixing — Lorenz and Coulomb Gauges: Because many different potential functions can produce the same physical electric and magnetic fields, we need to pick a specific convention (gauge) to make the potentials unique.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_12.text: Electromagnetic Duality: In vacuum, Maxwell's equations have a hidden symmetry: you can rotate electric and magnetic fields into each other, and the physics does not change.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_13.text: Debye Shielding: In a plasma or salt solution, a charged particle attracts opposite charges and repels like charges, forming a cloud that screens its electric field.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_14.text: Waveguide Theory (Guided Electromagnetic Waves): Waveguides channel electromagnetic waves along a defined path, just as pipes carry water.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_15.text: Antenna Theory (Radiation from Current Distributions): Antenna theory describes how electric currents radiate electromagnetic waves into space and how antennas receive them.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_16.text: London Equations (Superconducting Electrodynamics): Superconductors have zero electrical resistance and expel magnetic fields from their interior (the Meissner effect).

02_physics_electromagnetism_law_5.english: The Evanescent Wave and Near-Field Electrodynamics
02_physics_electromagnetism_law_5.text: The Evanescent Wave and Near-Field Electrodynamics: When light bounces off an internal surface at a steep angle (total internal reflection), it does not simply stop at the boundary.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_17.english: Plasma Oscillations and Electromagnetic Wave Dispersion in Plasmas
02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_17.text: Plasma Oscillations and Electromagnetic Wave Dispersion in Plasmas: Plasmas are collections of free electrons and ions that respond collectively to electromagnetic disturbances.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_18.english: Electromagnetic Radiation Pressure and Momentum Transfer
02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_18.text: Electromagnetic Radiation Pressure and Momentum Transfer: Light pushes on things.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_19.text: Transformation Optics and Electromagnetic Cloaking: Transformation optics uses the mathematical equivalence between curved space and material properties to design devices that control light in extraordinary ways.

02_physics_electromagnetism_principle_20.text: Casimir Effect (Vacuum Electromagnetic Fluctuations): Even in a perfect vacuum, quantum mechanics predicts that electromagnetic fields fluctuate randomly.

fp_02_physics_electromagnetism_principle.english: Topological Electromagnetism and Photonic Topological Insulators
fp_02_physics_electromagnetism_principle.text: Topological Electromagnetism and Photonic Topological Insulators: Topological photonics applies ideas from topological insulators to light.
Non-Hermitian Physics in Electromagnetism: Altermagnetism is a newly discovered type of magnetism that breaks the century-old classification of magnets into just ferromagnets and antiferromagnets.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_1.text: The Continuum Hypothesis: We treat fluids as smooth, continuous substances rather than collections of individual molecules.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_law_1.text: Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation): Mass is neither created nor destroyed.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_law_2.text: The Navier-Stokes Equations (Conservation of Momentum): Newton's second law for fluids: the acceleration of a fluid element equals the sum of pressure forces, viscous forces, and body forces (like gravity).

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_2.text: The Reynolds Number: The Reynolds number measures the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_3.text: Bernoulli's Principle: Where fluid flows faster, the pressure is lower.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_4.text: Turbulence: Turbulence is the chaotic, swirling motion seen in fast-flowing rivers, smoke plumes, and atmospheric storms.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_5.text: Vorticity and Circulation: Vorticity measures how much the fluid spins at each point.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_law_3.text: Euler Equations for Ideal Fluids: For fluids with negligible viscosity, the only forces are pressure and gravity.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_law_4.text: Stokes' Law (Low Reynolds Number Drag): A small, slow-moving sphere in a thick fluid feels a drag force proportional to its speed, its size, and the fluid's viscosity.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_6.text: Boundary Layer Theory (Prandtl): When a fast-moving fluid hits a surface, the "stickiness" (viscosity) only matters in a very thin layer near the surface.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_7.text: Potential Flow and Irrotational Motion: When a fluid has no rotation (no swirling), its flow can be described by a single potential function satisfying a well-studied equation — making many problems solvable in closed form.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_8.english: Dimensional Analysis and Similarity in Fluids
02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_8.text: Dimensional Analysis and Similarity in Fluids: Two fluid flows that "look the same" in dimensionless terms behave identically — this is why wind tunnel models work.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_theorem_1.text: Helmholtz Vortex Theorems: In an ideal fluid, vortices are remarkably persistent structures.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_9.text: Rayleigh-Taylor Instability: When heavy fluid sits on top of light fluid, the configuration is unstable — like trying to balance a book on top of a ball.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_10.text: Compressible Flow and Shock Waves: When an object moves faster than sound, it creates a shock wave — an abrupt, discontinuous change in pressure, density, and temperature.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_law_5.text: The No-Slip Condition: Fluid directly touching a solid surface does not slip along it — it sticks.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_11.text: The Kutta-Joukowski Theorem (Aerodynamic Lift): The lift on a wing is directly proportional to the circulation (net swirling flow) around it.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_12.text: The Vorticity Equation: The vorticity equation describes how swirling motion (vorticity) is created, stretched, tilted, and diffused in a fluid.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_13.text: Stokes Flow (Creeping Motion): At very low Reynolds numbers — the world of bacteria, sperm cells, and settling dust particles — viscosity completely dominates over inertia.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_14.text: Taylor-Couette Instability: When the inner cylinder spins fast enough, the smooth circular flow between two cylinders breaks up into a beautiful stack of donut-shaped vortex rolls.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_15.english: The Dean Number and Secondary Flows in Curved Channels
02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_15.text: The Dean Number and Secondary Flows in Curved Channels: When fluid flows through a curved pipe (like a garden hose coiled in a loop), the faster-moving fluid near the center is flung outward by centrifugal force, while slower fluid near the walls is pushed inward.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_16.text: Shallow Water Equations: When a layer of water is much wider than it is deep (oceans, rivers, dam breaks), vertical motion can be neglected and the flow is described by the shallow water equations.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_17.text: Microfluidics and Low-Reynolds-Number Transport: At the microscale, fluids behave very differently from everyday experience.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_18.text: Magnetohydrodynamic Dynamo Theory: The dynamo problem explains how planets, stars, and galaxies generate their magnetic fields.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_19.text: Viscoelastic Fluid Dynamics (Oldroyd-B and Beyond): Ordinary (Newtonian) fluids like water resist deformation through viscosity alone.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_20.text: Lattice Boltzmann Method: Instead of solving the Navier-Stokes equations directly, the lattice Boltzmann method simulates fluid flow by tracking imaginary particles hopping on a grid.

02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_21.english: Active Fluids and Biological Fluid Mechanics
02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_21.text: Active Fluids and Biological Fluid Mechanics: Active fluids are liquids full of swimming organisms or self-propelled particles.

fp_02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_1.text: Active Matter Hydrodynamics: Active matter physics describes collections of self-propelled entities -- swimming bacteria, crawling cells, flocking birds, vibrated grains -- that consume energy internally rather than being driven by external forces.

fp_02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_1.english: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Fluid Mechanics
fp_02_physics_fluid_dynamics_principle_1.text: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Fluid Mechanics: Elastic turbulence is a remarkable phenomenon where adding tiny amounts of polymer to a fluid causes chaotic, turbulent-like flow even when the fluid is moving extremely slowly — conditions under which ordinary fluids flow perfectly smoothly.

02_physics_optics_principle_1.text: Fermat's Principle (Principle of Least Time): Light always takes the path that minimizes travel time.

02_physics_optics_law_1.text: Snell's Law of Refraction: When light passes from one medium to another, it bends.

02_physics_optics_principle_2.english: The Wave Nature of Light (Huygens' Principle)
02_physics_optics_principle_2.text: The Wave Nature of Light (Huygens' Principle): Light propagates as a wave.

02_physics_optics_principle_3.text: Superposition and Interference: Waves add together.

02_physics_optics_principle_4.text: Photons and Quantum Optics: Light comes in individual particles (photons) whose energy is proportional to their frequency.

02_physics_optics_law_2.text: Planck's Radiation Law: A hot object radiates light at all frequencies, with a characteristic spectrum that depends only on temperature.

02_physics_optics_principle_5.text: Diffraction (Fraunhofer and Fresnel): Light bends around obstacles and spreads through narrow openings.

02_physics_optics_principle_6.text: Polarization of Light: Light waves vibrate in specific directions perpendicular to their travel.

02_physics_optics_law_3.text: Brewster's Angle: At a special angle of incidence, the reflected light becomes perfectly polarized — only the component vibrating parallel to the surface bounces back.

02_physics_optics_principle_7.text: Coherence: For interference to be visible, the light waves must be "in step" with each other.

02_physics_optics_principle_8.text: Abbe Diffraction Limit: There is a fundamental limit to how small an object a microscope (or any optical system) can resolve, set by the wavelength of light.

02_physics_optics_principle_9.text: Kramers-Kronig Relations: If you know how a material absorbs light at all frequencies, you can calculate how it bends light at any frequency, and vice versa.

02_physics_optics_theorem_1.text: The Optical Theorem: The total amount of scattering (in all directions) from an object is completely determined by how much the forward-scattered wave interferes with the incident wave.

02_physics_optics_law_4.text: Fresnel Equations: When light hits a surface between two materials, some reflects and some transmits.

02_physics_optics_principle_10.text: Nonlinear Optics: At very high light intensities, materials respond nonlinearly to light.

02_physics_optics_principle_11.english: Gaussian Beam Optics and the ABCD Matrix Formalism
02_physics_optics_principle_11.text: Gaussian Beam Optics and the ABCD Matrix Formalism: Laser beams are not perfectly parallel — they spread due to diffraction.

02_physics_optics_principle_12.text: Photonic Crystals and Photonic Bandgaps: Just as the periodic arrangement of atoms in a crystal creates energy bands and bandgaps for electrons, a periodic arrangement of different dielectric materials creates frequency bands and bandgaps for light.

02_physics_optics_principle_13.english: Squeezed States and Quantum Noise Limits
02_physics_optics_principle_13.text: Squeezed States and Quantum Noise Limits: Even a perfect laser beam has quantum noise — irreducible fluctuations imposed by the uncertainty principle.

02_physics_optics_principle_14.text: Metamaterials and Negative Refraction: Metamaterials are engineered structures that bend light in ways no natural material can.

02_physics_optics_principle_15.text: Fourier Optics: A lens naturally performs Fourier transforms on light.

02_physics_optics_principle_16.text: Adaptive Optics: Earth's atmosphere makes stars twinkle and blurs astronomical images.

02_physics_optics_principle_17.text: Orbital Angular Momentum of Light: Light can "twist" as it travels through space, carrying a corkscrew-like orbital angular momentum separate from its spin (polarization).

02_physics_optics_principle_18.text: Ghost Imaging and Quantum Imaging: Ghost imaging creates a picture of an object using light that never directly interacts with the imaging detector in the usual way.

02_physics_optics_principle_19.text: Attosecond Optics and High-Harmonic Generation: Attosecond optics creates the shortest controlled bursts of light ever produced -- lasting billionths of a billionth of a second.

02_physics_optics_principle_20.english: Structured Light (Spatial Light Modulation and Vector Beams)
02_physics_optics_principle_20.text: Structured Light (Spatial Light Modulation and Vector Beams): Instead of using light as a simple, uniform beam, structured light sculpts every property of a light beam -- its shape, its twist, its polarization pattern -- point by point across the beam.

02_physics_optics_principle_21.text: Optical Frequency Combs: An optical frequency comb is like a ruler for light, with millions of perfectly spaced "teeth" spanning the visible and infrared spectrum.

02_physics_optics_principle_22.text: Quantum Key Distribution (BB84 Protocol): Quantum key distribution uses the quantum properties of photons to create an unbreakable encryption key.

fp_02_physics_optics_principle_17.text: Topological Photonics: Topological photonics engineers light-guiding structures where the flow of light is protected by topology -- the global mathematical structure of the photonic band structure.

fp_02_physics_optics_principle_18.english: Orbital Angular Momentum of Light and Structured Light
