The Axiom of Projective Determinacy (PD): Projective sets are those definable from open sets by taking complements, projections, and countable unions/intersections -- a natural hierarchy of "definable" sets of real numbers.

07_formal_sciences_set_theory_principle_.english: Homotopy Type Theory and Univalent Foundations
07_formal_sciences_set_theory_principle_.text: Homotopy Type Theory and Univalent Foundations: Homotopy Type Theory provides an alternative foundation for mathematics where the basic objects are not sets but "shapes" (homotopy types).

Generic Absoluteness and Forcing Axioms: Forcing axioms say: "generic objects that could exist (in some forcing extension) already exist in the actual universe." These are the strongest axioms consistent with ZFC (short of large cardinals) and they settle many questions that ZFC alone cannot.

07_formal_sciences_set_theory_principle_.english: Inner Model Theory and Core Models
07_formal_sciences_set_theory_principle_.text: Inner Model Theory and Core Models: Many mathematical statements are independent of the standard axioms — they can be made true or false by choosing different models.

07_formal_sciences_systems_theory_princi.english: A System Is More Than the Sum of Its Parts (Emergence)
07_formal_sciences_systems_theory_princi.text: A System Is More Than the Sum of Its Parts (Emergence): When parts interact, the whole exhibits properties that none of the parts possess individually.
Feedback: Negative feedback = thermostat (corrects deviations).
Homeostasis and Equilibrium: Stable systems resist change and return to their normal state after being disturbed.

07_formal_sciences_systems_theory_princi.english: The Principle of Requisite Variety (Ashby's Law)
07_formal_sciences_systems_theory_princi.text: The Principle of Requisite Variety (Ashby's Law): Only variety can absorb variety.
Self-Organization: Order can arise from disorder without a central controller — pattern emerges from the interactions of components following simple local rules.
Hierarchy and Modularity: Complex systems are built in layers, like nested boxes.

07_formal_sciences_systems_theory_princi.english: Entropy, Dissipative Structures, and Open Systems
07_formal_sciences_systems_theory_princi.text: Entropy, Dissipative Structures, and Open Systems: Left alone, things fall apart (entropy increases).
Network Theory: Many real-world systems — social networks, the internet, neural networks, ecosystems — are organized as networks with specific structural properties.
Autopoiesis: A living cell creates its own membrane and internal machinery, which in turn keep the cell alive to create more membrane and machinery.

07_formal_sciences_systems_theory_princi.english: Attractors and Stability in Dynamical Systems
07_formal_sciences_systems_theory_princi.text: Attractors and Stability in Dynamical Systems: Complex systems tend to settle into characteristic patterns of behavior — called attractors.
Cybernetics and Control Theory: Cybernetics studies how systems regulate themselves through feedback and information.
Resilience and Adaptive Cycles: Resilience is not about resisting change but about being able to absorb shocks and still function.

Viable System Model (Beer): Any organization that wants to survive must have five essential functions: doing the work, coordinating the parts, managing resources, watching the environment, and maintaining identity.
Soft Systems Methodology (Checkland): Many real-world problems are not well-defined engineering problems but messy human situations where people disagree about what the problem even is.

System Dynamics (Forrester): System dynamics builds simulation models of complex systems using stocks (things that accumulate, like water in a bathtub), flows (rates of filling and draining), and feedback loops.
Complex Adaptive Systems: Complex adaptive systems are collections of many interacting agents that learn and adapt.

07_formal_sciences_systems_theory_theore.text: Conant-Ashby Theorem (Good Regulator Theorem): To control something well, you must have an internal model of it.

07_formal_sciences_systems_theory_princi.text: Catastrophe Theory: Catastrophe theory studies how smooth, gradual changes in conditions can cause sudden, dramatic shifts in behavior.
Anticipatory Systems: Anticipatory systems act based on predictions of their own future, not just reactions to the present.
Multilevel / Multi-Scale Systems: Real systems operate simultaneously at multiple scales: molecules form cells, cells form organs, organs form organisms, organisms form ecosystems.
Network Science and Multilayer Networks: Real-world networks are not isolated: the power grid depends on the communication network, which depends on the power grid.

Digital Twin Concept and Theory: A digital twin is a living virtual copy of a real physical system -- an engine, a bridge, a patient, or even a city -- that updates itself continuously with real-world data.

System of Systems Engineering (SoSE): Many of the most important and challenging systems in the modern world are not single systems but "systems of systems": the internet is a system of systems (independently operated networks cooperating), as are military command structures, smart grids, healthcare systems, and autonomous vehicle fleets.

Agent-Based Modeling Foundations: Instead of writing equations for the whole system, agent-based modeling builds the system from the bottom up: define simple rules for how individuals behave, let them interact, and watch what emerges.
Viable System Model (Beer): If you randomly remove pieces from a network, at what point does it fall apart?

08_social_sciences_anthropology_principl.text: Cultural Relativism: To understand another culture, you must step outside your own assumptions and see the world through their eyes.
Participant Observation (Ethnographic Method): To understand a culture, you must live in it.
Kinship Systems: Every society organizes itself around relationships of kinship -- who is related to whom and what that means.
The Gift and Exchange Theory (Mauss): In most human societies, exchanging gifts is not just about the stuff -- it is about the relationships.
Structural Analysis (Levi-Strauss): Beneath the surface diversity of human cultures, there are deep patterns.
Holism and the Four-Field Approach: Anthropology tries to understand everything about being human -- our biology, our cultures, our languages, and our past.
Liminality (Turner): In every culture, there are rituals that mark major life transitions: becoming an adult, getting married, being initiated into a group.
Thick Description (Geertz): Anthropology is not just about describing what people do -- it is about understanding what it means to them.
Mode of Production (Marx): How a society produces its livelihood determines its social structure, politics, and culture.
Ethnocentrism: Ethnocentrism is judging other cultures by your own standards -- assuming that the way your culture does things is the normal, natural, or best way.

Biological vs. Cultural Evolution: Humans evolve in two ways: biologically (through genes, over thousands of years) and culturally (through learning and teaching, which can happen in a single generation).
Structural Functionalism (Radcliffe-Brown): Structural functionalism asks: what does this custom or institution do for society?

Practice Theory (Bourdieu's Habitus): We act the way we do not because we follow explicit rules or make conscious calculations, but because our upbringing and social position have given us a "feel for the game" — a set of deeply ingrained habits, tastes, and dispositions (habitus).
Cultural Materialism (Harris): To understand why people believe what they believe and organize their societies the way they do, start with how they make a living and how they reproduce.
Multi-Sited Ethnography (Marcus): Traditional anthropology studied one village in depth.
Post-Colonial Anthropology: Anthropology has a colonial past.
Medical Anthropology: Design anthropology brings anthropologists' skills -- deep observation, understanding context, questioning assumptions -- into the world of design.
Forensic Anthropology: Forensic anthropology uses knowledge of the human skeleton to help solve crimes, identify disaster victims, and document human rights violations.

Digital Anthropology: When mass graves are discovered in Guatemala, Argentina, or Bosnia, forensic anthropologists are called to identify the dead — giving names back to victims of political violence and providing evidence for courts.

08_social_sciences_economics_axiom_1.text: Scarcity: There is not enough of everything for everyone.

08_social_sciences_economics_law_2.text: Supply and Demand: When the price of something goes up, people want less of it (demand falls) and producers want to make more of it (supply rises).

08_social_sciences_economics_principle_3.text: Rational Choice Theory: People have consistent preferences and make choices to get the best outcome they can afford.

08_social_sciences_economics_law_4.english: Diminishing Marginal Returns (and Diminishing Marginal Utility)
08_social_sciences_economics_law_4.text: Diminishing Marginal Returns (and Diminishing Marginal Utility): The more of something you have, the less additional benefit you get from one more unit.

08_social_sciences_economics_theorem_5.text: Comparative Advantage (Ricardo): Even if you are better at everything than your trading partner, you both benefit from trading if you each focus on what you are relatively best at.

08_social_sciences_economics_principle_6.english: General Equilibrium and the Welfare Theorems
08_social_sciences_economics_principle_6.text: General Equilibrium and the Welfare Theorems: Under idealized conditions (perfect competition, no externalities, complete information), markets automatically reach an efficient outcome -- one where you cannot make anyone better off without making someone else worse off.

08_social_sciences_economics_principle_7.text: Market Failures and Externalities: Markets sometimes fail to produce efficient outcomes.

08_social_sciences_economics_theorem_8.text: Coase Theorem: If people can bargain freely and cheaply, they will negotiate their way to the efficient outcome no matter who starts with the legal right.

08_social_sciences_economics_principle_9.text: Moral Hazard and Adverse Selection: Adverse selection: when buyers cannot tell good from bad, the bad drives out the good.

08_social_sciences_economics_law_10.text: Phillips Curve: In the short run, lower unemployment tends to come with higher inflation (more spending chases limited goods).

08_social_sciences_economics_theorem_11.text: Ricardian Equivalence: It does not matter whether the government taxes you now or borrows now and taxes you later -- if you are rational and farsighted, you save the money from a tax cut because you know future taxes must rise to repay the debt.

08_social_sciences_economics_principle_1.text: Efficient Market Hypothesis: You cannot consistently beat the market because prices already incorporate all available information.

08_social_sciences_economics_theorem_13.text: Nash Equilibrium: A Nash equilibrium is a situation where every player is doing the best they can given what everyone else is doing, so no one has a reason to change.

08_social_sciences_economics_theorem_14.text: Modigliani-Miller Theorem: How a firm pays for its operations -- debt versus equity -- does not affect its total value under ideal conditions.

08_social_sciences_economics_theorem_15.text: Heckscher-Ohlin Model: Countries export what they are naturally equipped to produce.

08_social_sciences_economics_principle_1.text: Keynesian Multiplier and Aggregate Demand: When the government spends an extra dollar, it does not just add one dollar to the economy -- it adds more, because the person who receives that dollar spends part of it, and the person who receives that spending spends part of it, and so on.
Solow Growth Model: Why do economies grow?

08_social_sciences_economics_law_18.text: Taylor Rule: Standard economics treats the environment as a subset of the economy — a source of resources and a sink for waste.

08_social_sciences_linguistics_axiom_1.text: Arbitrariness of the Sign (Saussure): There is no reason why a dog is called "dog" in English and "chien" in French.

08_social_sciences_linguistics_principle.text: Compositionality (Frege's Principle): You can understand sentences you have never heard before because you know the meanings of the individual words and the grammatical rules for combining them.

08_social_sciences_linguistics_hypothesi.text: Universal Grammar (Chomsky): Humans are born with a built-in blueprint for language.

08_social_sciences_linguistics_theorem_4.text: Chomsky Hierarchy of Formal Languages: Languages (in the mathematical sense of sets of strings) come in layers of complexity.

08_social_sciences_linguistics_principle.text: Pragmatic Principles (Grice's Maxims): Communication works because speakers and listeners cooperate.
Structural Levels of Linguistic Analysis: Language is organized in layers, like an onion.

08_social_sciences_linguistics_hypothesi.text: Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis (Linguistic Relativity): Does the language you speak shape how you think?

08_social_sciences_linguistics_principle.text: Merge Operation (Minimalism): All the complex structure of human language -- sentences within sentences, questions, passives, relative clauses -- may arise from a single, recursive operation: take two things and combine them.
Optimality Theory: Language follows rules, but rules can conflict.

Language Acquisition Device (LAD): Children learn language with remarkable speed and accuracy despite receiving imperfect input, without formal lessons, and at an age when they cannot tie their shoes.
Phonological Universals: Despite speaking 7,000+ different languages, humans use a remarkably similar set of sound building blocks.

08_social_sciences_linguistics_principle.english: Historical Linguistics and the Comparative Method
08_social_sciences_linguistics_principle.text: Historical Linguistics and the Comparative Method: Languages change over time in regular, predictable ways.
Speech Act Theory (Austin, Searle): Saying "I promise to be there" is not just describing a state of affairs — it is the act of promising.
Morphological Typology: Languages differ dramatically in how they build words.
Language Contact and Creolization: When people who speak different languages must communicate, new languages can emerge.

Construction Grammar: Instead of a rule-generating grammar plus a dictionary of words, construction grammar says language is a big collection of patterns -- from individual words to abstract sentence templates -- each pairing a form with a meaning.
Discourse Analysis: How can computers process, understand, and generate human language?
Sign Language Linguistics: Sign languages are not simply gesture systems or manual codes for spoken languages.
Documentary Linguistics: Language follows mathematical laws with surprising precision.

08_social_sciences_political_science_axi.text: Sovereignty: Sovereignty means "who is ultimately in charge." A sovereign state is the highest authority in its territory -- no one can overrule it domestically, and no foreign power has the right to interfere.

08_social_sciences_political_science_pri.text: Social Contract Theory: Why should anyone obey the government?
Separation of Powers: Do not give all power to one person or group.

Collective Action Problem: If everyone benefits from clean air, but cleaning up is costly and no one can be excluded from breathing clean air, then everyone has an incentive to let others do the work.

08_social_sciences_political_science_the.text: Median Voter Theorem: In a majority-rule election on a single issue, the winning position is the one preferred by the voter in the middle.
Arrow's Impossibility Theorem: There is no perfect voting system.

08_social_sciences_political_science_pri.text: Institutional Design and Path Dependence: The rules matter.
Selectorate Theory: Political leaders stay in power by keeping their essential supporters happy.
Democratic Peace Theory: Democracies almost never fight wars against other democracies.

Prisoner's Dilemma in International Relations: Nations face situations where each country is tempted to cheat (arm while others disarm, protect its industry while demanding free trade), but if everyone cheats, everyone is worse off.
Veto Player Theory: The more people (or institutions) who can block a policy change, and the more they disagree, the harder it is to change anything.

08_social_sciences_political_science_law.text: Duverger's Law: In countries where each district elects one representative and the most votes win (like the US and UK), you end up with two major parties.

08_social_sciences_political_science_the.text: Condorcet Paradox (Voting Cycles): Even if every individual voter has perfectly rational preferences, the group can end up going in circles.

08_social_sciences_political_science_pri.text: Power Transition Theory: The most dangerous moment in international politics is when a rising power is about to overtake the dominant one.

08_social_sciences_political_science_the.text: Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem: No fair voting system is immune to strategic voting.

08_social_sciences_political_science_pri.text: Hegemonic Stability Theory: Voting alone does not make a democracy legitimate -- what matters is the quality of the public conversation that precedes the vote.
Computational Social Science: The digital age generates vast amounts of data about human behavior: every tweet, search, purchase, and phone call leaves a trace.

08_social_sciences_political_science_pri.english: AI Governance and the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence
08_social_sciences_political_science_pri.text: AI Governance and the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence: As AI systems become more powerful and pervasive -- making decisions about hiring, criminal justice, medical diagnosis, and even military targeting -- the question of how to govern them becomes urgent.

08_social_sciences_political_science_pri.english: Quadratic Voting and Mechanism Design for Democracy
08_social_sciences_political_science_pri.text: Quadratic Voting and Mechanism Design for Democracy: In standard voting, everyone gets one vote regardless of how much they care about an issue.
Polarization Dynamics and Affective Polarization: Why do authoritarian regimes persist?

08_social_sciences_psychology_principle_.text: Classical Conditioning (Pavlovian Conditioning): If a bell always rings before food appears, eventually the bell alone makes you salivate.
Operant Conditioning (Instrumental Conditioning): We do more of what gets rewarded and less of what gets punished.

08_social_sciences_psychology_law_3.text: Weber-Fechner Law (Psychophysics): You notice changes relative to what you already have.

08_social_sciences_psychology_principle_.text: Information Processing Model of Cognition: The mind works somewhat like a computer: information comes in through the senses, gets filtered by attention, held briefly in working memory (which has strict capacity limits), and either forgotten or stored in long-term memory.
Cognitive Dissonance Theory: When your actions contradict your beliefs, you feel uncomfortable.

Dual-Process Theory of Cognition: You have two ways of thinking: a fast, intuitive mode that operates automatically (gut feelings, snap judgments) and a slow, deliberate mode that requires effort (careful analysis, logical reasoning).
Developmental Stages and Critical Periods: Children's minds develop in stages, not just by accumulating more knowledge but by changing how they think.
Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs: You worry about food before friendship, and friendship before finding your purpose.
Piaget's Stages of Cognitive Development: Children do not just know less than adults -- they think differently.
Attachment Theory (Bowlby): The bond between an infant and caregiver is not just emotional comfort -- it shapes how the child relates to others for the rest of their life.
Prospect Theory (Kahneman-Tversky): Losing $100 hurts about twice as much as gaining $100 feels good.
Working Memory Model (Baddeley): Working memory is your mental workspace -- the limited amount of information you can hold in mind and manipulate at any moment.
Fundamental Attribution Error: When someone cuts you off in traffic, you think "what a jerk" (personality).
Social Identity Theory (Tajfel): A large part of who you think you are comes from the groups you belong to -- your nation, team, profession, religion.
Learned Helplessness (Seligman): If you repeatedly experience situations where nothing you do makes a difference, you stop trying -- even when the situation changes and you could succeed.

08_social_sciences_psychology_law_16.text: Yerkes-Dodson Law: A little stress helps you perform; too much stress makes you choke.

08_social_sciences_psychology_principle_.text: Obedience to Authority (Milgram): Ordinary people will do terrible things when told to by someone in authority.

Bandura's Social Learning Theory: ACT starts from a radical premise: trying to control or eliminate painful thoughts and feelings is not just ineffective — it is often the cause of psychological problems.

08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_1.text: Social Facts (Durkheim): Society is more than the sum of its individuals.

08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_2.text: Structuration Theory (Giddens): Society shapes people, and people shape society -- simultaneously.

08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_3.text: Symbolic Interactionism: Society is built from the bottom up through face-to-face interactions.

08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_4.text: Social Stratification and Inequality: Every society has a hierarchy.

08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_5.text: Collective Behavior and Social Movements: Sometimes people come together to change society -- through protests, revolutions, or movements.

08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_6.english: Socialization and the Social Construction of Reality
08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_6.text: Socialization and the Social Construction of Reality: You were not born knowing how to behave in your society; you were taught, gradually and thoroughly, from birth.

08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_7.text: Anomie (Durkheim): When the rules of society break down -- during rapid change, economic crisis, or social upheaval -- people lose their moral compass.

08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_8.text: Iron Cage (Weber): Modern society traps us in bureaucratic systems designed for efficiency and control.

08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_9.text: Bourdieu's Capitals: Inequality is not just about money.

08_social_sciences_sociology_principle_1.text: Network Theory (Granovetter's Weak Ties): It is not your close friends but your acquaintances who are most likely to help you find a job.
Labeling Theory: Who gets called a "criminal" depends less on what they did and more on who is doing the labeling.
Historical Materialism (Marx): Marx argued that economics is the foundation of everything in society.

Rational Choice Theory in Sociology (Coleman): Rational choice theory explains social patterns by asking: what are individuals trying to achieve, and how do their individual choices add up to produce collective outcomes?
Dramaturgical Analysis (Goffman): Social life is a performance.
Mechanical and Organic Solidarity (Durkheim): In small traditional societies, everyone is alike and cohesion comes from sameness -- shared beliefs, rituals, and customs.
Ideal Types (Weber): An ideal type is a simplified mental model that exaggerates the key features of a social phenomenon to make it easier to study.
Intersectionality (Crenshaw): You cannot understand someone's social position by looking at just one thing -- race, or gender, or class -- in isolation.
World-Systems Theory (Wallerstein): Why do Germany and the United States have such different economic systems — and why do both succeed?

09_philosophy_of_science_epistemology_pr.english: Justified True Belief (The Classical Analysis of Knowledge)
09_philosophy_of_science_epistemology_pr.text: Justified True Belief (The Classical Analysis of Knowledge): Knowledge is not just believing something true; you need good reasons for your belief.
The Problem of Induction (Hume): Science relies on reasoning from what we have observed to what we have not yet observed (induction).
Falsificationism (Popper): A scientific theory must stick its neck out: it must make predictions that could be proved wrong.
Paradigm Shifts (Kuhn): Science does not progress steadily by accumulating truths.

09_philosophy_of_science_epistemology_pr.english: Underdetermination of Theory by Evidence (Quine-Duhem Thesis)
09_philosophy_of_science_epistemology_pr.text: Underdetermination of Theory by Evidence (Quine-Duhem Thesis): Evidence alone cannot tell you which theory is right.

Bayesian Epistemology and Confirmation: Bayesian epistemology says that rational belief comes in degrees (not just "believe" or "do not believe") and that you should update your beliefs in light of new evidence using Bayes' theorem.

09_philosophy_of_science_epistemology_pr.english: Justified True Belief and Gettier Problems
09_philosophy_of_science_epistemology_pr.text: Justified True Belief and Gettier Problems: Gettier showed that you can have a well-justified, true belief and still not really "know" something -- because you are right for the wrong reasons.
Foundationalism vs Coherentism: When you justify a belief, you give a reason.
Empiricism (Locke, Hume): All real knowledge about the world comes from the senses.
Rationalism (Descartes, Leibniz): Some things can be known by thinking, not just by looking.

09_philosophy_of_science_epistemology_pr.english: Skepticism and the Problem of Induction (Hume)
09_philosophy_of_science_epistemology_pr.text: Skepticism and the Problem of Induction (Hume): The skeptic asks: how do you really know anything?
Reliabilism (Goldman): What makes a belief justified?
Contextualism: Whether it is true to say someone "knows" something depends on the conversation.

09_philosophy_of_science_epistemology_pr.english: Inference to the Best Explanation (IBE / Abduction)
09_philosophy_of_science_epistemology_pr.text: Inference to the Best Explanation (IBE / Abduction): When scientists consider competing explanations and choose the one that best fits the evidence, they are using inference to the best explanation.
Theory-Ladenness of Observation: There is no such thing as pure, unbiased observation.
Social Epistemology: Knowledge is not just an individual achievement -- it is a social product.
Epistemic Virtues and Virtue Epistemology: What makes someone a good knower is not just having true beliefs with good evidence, but being the right kind of thinker.
Naturalized Epistemology (Quine): Quine argued that traditional philosophy's attempts to justify all our knowledge from first principles were doomed to fail.
Epistemic Injustice (Fricker): Sometimes people are wronged not by being robbed or physically harmed, but by being denied the status of a knowledgeable person.
Knowledge-First Epistemology (Williamson): Feminist epistemology asks: does who you are affect what you can know?

09_philosophy_of_science_metaphysics_pri.text: Causation: What does it mean for one thing to cause another?
Laws of Nature: Are the laws of physics necessary truths about the universe, or are they just descriptions of patterns that happen to hold?
Natural Kinds: Is "gold" a real category in nature, or just a label we invented?
Identity and Persistence: Is the ship rebuilt plank by plank the same ship (Ship of Theseus)?
Mereology (Part-Whole Relations): When do parts make a whole?
Determinism and Indeterminism: Is the future completely determined by the present and the laws of physics, or is there genuine randomness in the world?
Ontological Categories (Substance, Property, Relation): What are the most basic kinds of things that exist?
Causation (Hume's Regularity, Counterfactual, Interventionist): What does it mean for one thing to cause another?
Personal Identity (Locke, Hume, Bundle Theory): What makes you the same person today as you were ten years ago?
Free Will (Compatibilism, Libertarianism, Hard Determinism): Do you have free will?
Possible Worlds (Lewis, Kripke): Could things have been different?
Universals vs Particulars (Plato, Aristotle, Nominalism): When we say two different roses are both red, is there one thing -- redness -- that they both share?
Supervenience: Supervenience captures the idea of dependence without identity.

Trope Theory: Instead of saying that two roses share the universal "redness," trope theory says each rose has its own particular redness -- a unique instance that resembles but is not identical to the other.
Modal Realism (Lewis): David Lewis took possible worlds literally.
Grounding: Grounding is the "in virtue of" relation.
Temporal Parts vs. Endurantism: When you say "that is the same cat I saw yesterday," what does "the same" mean?
Truthmaker Theory: If it is true that "the cat is on the mat," something in the world must make it true.
Dispositions and Categorical Properties: Is glass fragile because of some underlying structural property (categorical), or is fragility itself the fundamental nature of the glass?

Structural Realism (Metaphysical): Structural realism says that what science really discovers is the structure of the world -- the mathematical relationships between things -- not the intrinsic nature of the things themselves.
Abstract Objects (Ontology of Abstracta): Do numbers, sets, and propositions really exist?
Modality and Essence: Could things have been different?
Best Systems Account of Laws (Lewis-Ramsey): What is a property, fundamentally?

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_b.text: The Species Problem: What makes a group of organisms a species?
Levels of Selection: Does evolution select genes, organisms, groups, or all of the above?
Teleology and Function in Biology: Biology is full of purpose-talk: organs have functions, behaviors have goals, adaptations are "for" something.
Reduction vs. Autonomy of Biology: Can we explain everything in biology using just physics and chemistry?

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_b.english: The Gene Concept and Its Evolution
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_b.text: The Gene Concept and Its Evolution: What is a gene?
Adaptationism and Its Critics: Is every feature of an organism there because natural selection put it there?

Species Concepts (Biological, Phylogenetic, Morphological): There are over 26 proposed ways to define what a species is, but the big three are: can they breed together (biological), do they share exclusive common ancestry (phylogenetic), or do they look alike (morphological)?

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_b.english: Units of Selection Debate (Gene, Individual, Group)
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_b.text: Units of Selection Debate (Gene, Individual, Group): When natural selection acts, what is it really selecting -- genes, organisms, or groups?
Teleology and Function (Etiological vs Dispositional): When we say the function of the heart is to pump blood, what exactly do we mean?
Reduction vs Emergence in Biology: Can biology be fully explained by physics and chemistry?
Philosophy of Ecology (Community, Ecosystem Concepts): Is a forest community a real thing, like an organism, or just a collection of trees that happen to grow in the same place?
Evolutionary Epistemology (Knowledge as Evolved Adaptation): Our ability to know the world evolved.
Extended Evolutionary Synthesis: The standard theory of evolution (the Modern Synthesis) focuses on genes, natural selection, and random mutation.
Niche Construction: Organisms do not just adapt to their environments -- they change them.
Units of Selection Debate (Extended): What counts as a biological individual?
Biological Individuality: Where does one organism end and another begin?
Cultural Evolution Theory: Culture evolves.
Developmental Systems Theory: Genes do not contain a blueprint for the organism.
The Homology Concept: Your arm, a whale's flipper, and a bat's wing are all homologous -- they are the same structure inherited from a common ancestor, modified for different purposes.
Philosophy of Medicine: What counts as a disease?
The Fitness Concept: What does it mean to be "fit" in evolutionary biology?

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_b.english: Epigenetic Inheritance and the Lamarckism Debate
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_b.text: Epigenetic Inheritance and the Lamarckism Debate: Can the experiences of parents affect the genes of their children -- not by changing the DNA sequence, but by adding chemical tags that turn genes on or off?
Philosophy of Classification and Taxonomy: You are not just you — you are a community.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.text: Platonism (Mathematical Realism): Mathematical objects like the number 7 or the set of prime numbers really exist, in a realm separate from the physical world.
Formalism: Mathematics is a game of symbols.
Intuitionism and Constructivism: Mathematics is what we can build in our minds.
Structuralism: Mathematics is not about particular objects (like the number 2) but about patterns and structures.
The Indispensability Argument (Quine-Putnam): If math is essential to science, and science describes reality, then mathematical objects must be real.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.english: Mathematical Truth and the Question of Objectivity
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.text: Mathematical Truth and the Question of Objectivity: What makes "2 + 2 = 4" true?

Platonism (Mathematical Objects Exist Independently): Platonism says mathematical objects like the number pi, the Mandelbrot set, and infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces are just as real as tables and chairs -- but they exist outside space and time.

Formalism (Mathematics as Symbol Manipulation, Hilbert): Hilbert wanted to make mathematics safe by turning it into a perfectly rigorous game: define your axioms, state your rules, and then everything is just symbol manipulation.
Intuitionism (Mathematics as Mental Construction, Brouwer): Brouwer insisted that mathematics is a human mental activity, not a formal game or a description of an abstract Platonic realm.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.english: Structuralism (Mathematics is about Structures, not Objects)
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.text: Structuralism (Mathematics is about Structures, not Objects): Structuralism says that what matters in mathematics is not the objects themselves but the patterns and relationships between them.

Indispensability Argument (Quine-Putnam): If mathematics is essential to our best scientific theories, and we believe those theories describe reality, then we should believe mathematical objects are real too.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.english: Philosophy of Mathematical Practice (Lakatos, Proofs and Refutations)
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.text: Philosophy of Mathematical Practice (Lakatos, Proofs and Refutations): Philosophers have traditionally asked "What is mathematical truth?" and "Do numbers exist?" But a newer tradition asks: "How does mathematics actually work?" Lakatos showed that proofs evolve through a back-and-forth of conjectures and counterexamples, much like scientific theories.
Logicism (Frege/Russell): Logicism says that mathematics is just logic in disguise.
Fictionalism: Fictionalism says mathematical objects do not exist -- there are no numbers, sets, or functions out there in reality.
Predicativism: Predicativism is the view that mathematical definitions must not be circular.
Benacerraf's Dilemma: Benacerraf's dilemma is the deepest problem in the philosophy of mathematics.

Godel's Realism (Godel's Platonism): Godel believed that mathematical objects are just as real as physical objects, and that we can perceive them with our mathematical intuition -- a kind of inner sight.
The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Mathematics (Wigner): Why does mathematics work so well in physics?
Mathematical Explanation: Can mathematics itself explain why something happens, or does it just help us describe what happens?

Category Theory as Foundational Framework: Category theory is a kind of "mathematics of mathematics." Instead of starting with sets and building up, it starts with structures and the mappings between them.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.english: Mathematical Intuition and the Phenomenology of Mathematics
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.text: Mathematical Intuition and the Phenomenology of Mathematics: Mathematicians often report "seeing" that a theorem is true before they can prove it formally.
The Philosophy of Mathematical Proof: What counts as a proof?
Nominalism and Indispensability Responses: Why does mathematics work so well in physics?
The Mind-Body Problem: How is the mind related to the brain?
Functionalism: What makes something a belief or a desire is not what it is made of but what it does -- how it relates to perception, other mental states, and behavior.
The Hard Problem of Consciousness (Chalmers): Science can explain how the brain processes information, discriminates stimuli, and controls behavior.
Intentionality: Thoughts are about things.
Multiple Realizability: Pain in a human, an octopus, and a hypothetical robot could all be the same type of mental state even though the underlying physical mechanisms are completely different.
The Chinese Room Argument (Searle): A computer might perfectly simulate understanding -- giving the right answers to every question -- without actually understanding anything.
Supervenience and Mental Causation: Almost all philosophers agree that the mind depends on the brain: change the brain and you change the mind.
Mind-Body Problem (Dualism, Physicalism, Functionalism): The mind-body problem is philosophy's hardest question.
Consciousness: Hard Problem (Chalmers): Why does anything feel like anything?
Intentionality (Brentano, Aboutness of Mental States): Your thoughts are about things.

Multiple Realizability (Putnam, Against Type Identity): Pain in a human is one kind of brain state, pain in an octopus is a different kind, and a robot might feel pain through entirely different hardware.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.english: Chinese Room Argument (Searle, Against Strong AI)
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.text: Chinese Room Argument (Searle, Against Strong AI): Searle's thought experiment challenges the idea that a computer could ever truly understand anything.
Computational Theory of Mind (Fodor): Thinking is computing.
Qualia and the Knowledge Argument (Jackson): What is the specific feel of seeing the color red?
The Zombie Argument (Chalmers): Imagine a perfect atom-for-atom copy of you, but with no inner experience whatsoever -- no feelings, no consciousness, just complete darkness inside.
Higher-Order Theories of Consciousness: What makes a mental state conscious?
Integrated Information Theory (IIT / Phi): IIT says consciousness just is integrated information.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.english: Predictive Processing and the Free Energy Principle
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_m.text: Predictive Processing and the Free Energy Principle: Your brain is a prediction machine.
Eliminative Materialism: Eliminative materialism says our everyday understanding of the mind is fundamentally wrong.
Enactivism: Enactivism says the mind is not a computer processing information from the senses.
Panpsychism: Panpsychism says that consciousness is not something that magically appears at a certain level of complexity.
Extended Mind Thesis: Your mind is not just in your head.

Anomalous Monism (Davidson): Davidson tried to thread a needle: the mind is physical (every mental event is a brain event), yet there are no strict laws connecting types of thoughts to types of brain states.
Neural Correlates of Consciousness (NCC): Neuroscientists search for the brain activity that corresponds to conscious experience: when you see red, what specific neurons or brain regions are active?
Global Workspace Theory (Baars, Dehaene): Where does your mind end and the world begin?

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.english: The Measurement Problem and Interpretations of Quantum Mechanics
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.text: The Measurement Problem and Interpretations of Quantum Mechanics: Quantum mechanics is our most successful physical theory, but no one agrees on what it says about reality.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.english: The Nature of Spacetime (Substantivalism vs. Relationalism)
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.text: The Nature of Spacetime (Substantivalism vs. Relationalism): Is space a thing in itself -- a container that would exist even if there were nothing in it?
Determinism in Physics: Classical physics seemed to guarantee that the future is completely determined by the present.
Symmetry and Physical Reality: The deepest laws of physics are connected to symmetries.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.english: The Direction of Time and Statistical Mechanics
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.text: The Direction of Time and Statistical Mechanics: The laws of physics do not care about the direction of time -- they work the same forwards and backwards.
Scientific Realism and Quantum Mechanics: Does physics describe reality as it really is, or just predict what we will observe?

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.english: Measurement Problem in QM (Wave Function Collapse, Interpretations)
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.text: Measurement Problem in QM (Wave Function Collapse, Interpretations): Quantum mechanics' deepest problem is this: the math says that before you look, a particle can be in multiple states at once (superposition).

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.english: Problem of Time in Quantum Gravity
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.text: Problem of Time in Quantum Gravity: In quantum mechanics, time ticks in the background -- it is the stage on which physics plays out.
Conventionality of Simultaneity (Reichenbach, Grunbaum): In special relativity, you synchronize distant clocks by bouncing light between them.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.english: Symmetry and Invariance in Physics (Structural Realism)
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.text: Symmetry and Invariance in Physics (Structural Realism): The deepest lesson of modern physics may be that reality is structure, not stuff.
Arrow of Time (Entropic, Cosmological, Psychological): Time has a direction: eggs break but do not unbreak, we age but do not grow young, we remember yesterday but not tomorrow.
Determinism vs Indeterminism: Is the future written, or is there genuine randomness in the universe?

Bell's Theorem and Non-Locality: Bell proved that quantum mechanics cannot be explained by any theory where particles have definite properties before measurement and where nothing travels faster than light.
Black Hole Information Paradox: If you throw an encyclopedia into a black hole and it evaporates into featureless thermal radiation, where did the information go?
The Anthropic Principle: The universe seems suspiciously well-suited for life.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.english: Problem of Time in Quantum Gravity (Extended)
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.text: Problem of Time in Quantum Gravity (Extended): In our most fundamental theories, time vanishes.
The Measurement Problem and Decoherence: Decoherence explains why the quantum weirdness of superposition and interference disappears for large objects.
Mach's Principle: When you spin a bucket of water and the surface becomes concave, Newton said this shows the water accelerates relative to absolute space.

09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.english: CPT Symmetry and Its Philosophical Significance
09_philosophy_of_science_philosophy_of_p.text: CPT Symmetry and Its Philosophical Significance: The laws of physics are symmetric under a combined operation: swap every particle for its antiparticle, flip the spatial coordinates left-to-right, and reverse the direction of time.
Quantum Gravity Approaches: General relativity describes gravity as the curvature of spacetime.
Quantum Logic: Does quantum mechanics force us to change the rules of logic itself?
The Equivalence Principle (Philosophical Analysis): Einstein noticed that being in a gravitational field feels exactly like being in an accelerating elevator.
Underdetermination of Quantum Mechanical Interpretations: Modern physics is built on symmetry principles.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: Free Energy Landscapes: Every biological molecule exists in a landscape of energy states, like a ball rolling over hills and valleys.
Molecular Motors and Mechanochemical Coupling: Biological molecular motors are tiny machines that convert chemical fuel (ATP) into movement and force.
Membrane Biophysics: Cell membranes are thin sheets of fat molecules that form spontaneously because their structure minimizes energy in water.
Polymer Physics of Biomolecules: DNA and proteins are long, flexible chains, and their behavior can be understood using the same physics that describes synthetic polymers.

Single-Molecule Mechanics and Force Spectroscopy: We can now grab individual molecules and pull on them, measuring how much force it takes to unfold a protein, unzip a DNA double helix, or break a molecular bond.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.english: Electrostatics and Screening in Biological Systems
10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: Electrostatics and Screening in Biological Systems: Biological molecules are charged and operate in salty water.
Ion Channel Biophysics: Ion channels are the molecular gates that control electrical signaling in all cells.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.english: Diffusion and Transport in Biological Systems
10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: Diffusion and Transport in Biological Systems: Inside cells, molecules move by random thermal motion (diffusion).

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.english: Biomechanics and Elasticity of Biological Materials
10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: Biomechanics and Elasticity of Biological Materials: Biological structures are not rigid — they flex, stretch, and respond to forces.
Photosynthesis Biophysics: Plants capture sunlight using molecular antennae and transfer the energy with near-perfect efficiency to reaction centers that convert it to chemical fuel.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.english: Protein Folding and the Energy Landscape
10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: Protein Folding and the Energy Landscape: Proteins fold into precisely the right 3D shape out of an astronomical number of possibilities, and they do it in milliseconds.
Stochastic Processes in Biology: Inside cells, many molecules exist in tiny numbers — sometimes just a handful.
Kramers' Rate Theory: Kramers calculated how fast a molecule crosses an energy barrier due to thermal fluctuations.

Fluctuation Theorems (Jarzynski, Crooks): If you repeatedly pull apart a molecule, each time measuring the work, the Jarzynski equality lets you extract the exact equilibrium free energy difference from these non-equilibrium measurements.
Mechanotransduction: Cells feel their mechanical environment.

Liquid-Liquid Phase Separation (LLPS) in Cells: Cells organize themselves using liquid droplets -- certain proteins and RNAs spontaneously separate from the surrounding fluid to form dense condensates without needing a membrane.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.english: Cooperative Binding and the Hill Equation
10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: Cooperative Binding and the Hill Equation: Hemoglobin picks up oxygen cooperatively: the first oxygen molecule is hard to bind, but once it binds, the protein changes shape, making the next oxygen molecule easier to bind.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_law_18.text: Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET): FRET is a way to measure distances between molecules at the nanometer scale -- too small for microscopes to see directly.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: Patch-Clamp Electrophysiology: The patch-clamp technique lets you listen to a single ion channel opening and closing -- one molecule at a time.
Biological Pattern Formation (Turing Patterns): How does a leopard get its spots?
Optical Trapping Theory: Optical tweezers use a focused laser beam to grab and hold tiny objects -- even single molecules.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.english: Biological Noise and Stochastic Gene Expression (Detailed)
10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: Biological Noise and Stochastic Gene Expression (Detailed): Even genetically identical cells in the same environment can behave very differently because gene expression is inherently noisy.
Allosteric Regulation and Conformational Selection: Proteins are not rigid machines -- they breathe and fluctuate between multiple shapes.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.english: Macromolecular Crowding and Excluded Volume Effects
10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: Macromolecular Crowding and Excluded Volume Effects: Inside a cell, molecules are packed so tightly that there is barely room to move.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.english: DNA Nanotechnology and Structural DNA Design
10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: DNA Nanotechnology and Structural DNA Design: DNA is not just a carrier of genetic information -- it is the world's most programmable building material.
Active Matter Physics: Ordinary matter sits in equilibrium -- a gas in a box eventually reaches a uniform state.

10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.english: Protein Design and Inverse Folding (AI-Driven Biophysics)
10_interdisciplinary_biophysics_principl.text: Protein Design and Inverse Folding (AI-Driven Biophysics): Nature has spent billions of years evolving proteins -- molecular machines that do everything from digesting food to fighting infections.
Mechanobiology (Force as Biological Signal): Cells do not just respond to chemical signals -- they also feel and respond to mechanical forces.

Cryo-Electron Microscopy Revolution: For decades, determining the 3D structure of a protein required growing crystals -- which many important proteins (especially membrane proteins and large complexes) refuse to do.
Single-Molecule Force Spectroscopy (Advanced Techniques): How does a cell make a reliable decision when its internal signals are noisy and imprecise?

10_interdisciplinary_cognitive_science_p.text: The Computational Theory of Mind: Thinking is computing.
Embodied Cognition: You do not think with your brain alone -- you think with your whole body and the world around it.
Marr's Three Levels of Analysis: To understand how the brain (or a computer) does something, you need to answer three questions at three different levels: (1) What problem is it solving?
Bayesian Brain Hypothesis: The brain is a prediction machine.
Connectionism vs. Symbolicism: Is the mind more like a rule-following computer (processing symbols according to logic) or more like a brain (a network of simple elements that learns from experience)?
Modularity of Mind: Your mind is not a general-purpose computer.
Ecological Psychology (Gibson's Affordances): The world offers us possibilities for action — a chair affords sitting, a handle affords grasping.

10_interdisciplinary_cognitive_science_p.english: The Extended Mind and Distributed Cognition
10_interdisciplinary_cognitive_science_p.text: The Extended Mind and Distributed Cognition: Your smartphone, your notebook, and the people you work with are part of your cognitive system, not just tools you use.
Attention and Cognitive Bottlenecks: You can't process everything at once.
Memory Systems and Consolidation: You have different kinds of memory — memory for facts and events (declarative) and memory for skills and habits (procedural).
Language and Cognition: Humans are uniquely equipped for language.
Consciousness and the Hard Problem: Why does seeing red feel like something?
Global Workspace Theory: Many brain processes run unconsciously in parallel.
Dual-Process Theory (System 1 / System 2): You have two modes of thinking.
Theory of Mind: Theory of mind is the ability to understand that other people have their own thoughts and beliefs, which may differ from yours.
Neural Darwinism (Edelman): The brain is not programmed; it is selected.

Dynamic Systems Approach to Cognition: Instead of thinking of the mind as a computer running programs, the dynamic systems approach sees cognition as a continuously evolving physical process -- like a river flowing, not a program executing.
Mirror Neurons and Simulation Theory: Mirror neurons fire both when you do something and when you watch someone else do the same thing.

Cognitive Development as Dynamical Change: The brain appears to operate at a special balance point between two extremes: too much order (where neural activity dies out quickly and the brain cannot respond to inputs) and too much chaos (where neural activity explodes uncontrollably, as in a seizure).

10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.text: Emergence: The whole is more than the sum of its parts.
Self-Organized Criticality: Some systems naturally evolve to a tipping point where small events and large events are connected by the same dynamics.

Power Laws and Heavy-Tailed Distributions: In many natural and social systems, the distribution of event sizes follows a pattern where most events are small but a few are enormous -- and these extreme events are far more common than a bell curve would predict.

10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.english: Chaos and Sensitivity to Initial Conditions
10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.text: Chaos and Sensitivity to Initial Conditions: In a chaotic system, tiny differences in starting conditions grow exponentially over time, making long-term prediction impossible even though the system follows completely deterministic rules.

10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.english: Network Theory (Small Worlds and Scale-Free Networks)
10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.text: Network Theory (Small Worlds and Scale-Free Networks): Many real-world systems -- social networks, the Internet, biological networks, airline routes -- share surprising structural properties.
Cellular Automata and Computational Universality: Imagine a grid of cells, each either alive or dead.
Agent-Based Modeling: Instead of writing equations for the whole system, model each individual agent (person, cell, animal) and its rules, then let them interact.
Dissipative Structures and Far-From-Equilibrium Systems: Order can spontaneously arise from disorder when energy flows through a system.
The Edge of Chaos: Too much order means rigidity; too much chaos means instability.
Feedback Loops and Nonlinearity: When the output of a system feeds back to influence its own input, unexpected behaviors emerge.
Universality and Scaling Laws: Very different systems — magnets, fluids, economies, ecosystems — can behave in mathematically identical ways near tipping points.

10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.english: Adaptation and Evolution in Complex Systems
10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.text: Adaptation and Evolution in Complex Systems: Complex systems that can learn and evolve — organisms, markets, cultures, AI systems — share common principles: generate variation, select what works, and propagate the winners.
Fitness Landscapes: Imagine evolution as a hike through a mountainous landscape where height represents fitness.
Renormalization Group in Complexity: The renormalization group is a powerful tool for understanding how systems look at different scales.
Information Integration and Complexity Measures: What makes a system truly complex, as opposed to merely random or merely ordered?
Percolation Theory: Imagine a grid where each cell is randomly filled or empty.
Boolean Networks (Kauffman): Kauffman asked: what if you connected genes randomly, with each gene controlled by a few others, and let the network run?

Network Robustness and Vulnerability: The internet, airline networks, and cellular networks are all organized around a few highly connected "hubs." This makes them remarkably resilient to random failures (most of the time, only minor nodes go down), but catastrophically vulnerable to targeted attacks on hubs.
Evolutionary Computation: Can we solve hard problems by simulating evolution on a computer?

Multilayer and Multiplex Networks: Real networks are not simple: people are connected by friendship, work, and family simultaneously; infrastructure systems (power, internet, water) depend on each other.

10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.english: Reservoir Computing and Echo State Networks
10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.text: Reservoir Computing and Echo State Networks: What if you could compute with any complex dynamical system?
Tipping Points and Critical Transitions: Some systems do not change gradually -- they can suddenly flip from one state to a completely different one.
Stigmergy and Swarm Intelligence: How do thousands of ants find the shortest path to food without a leader, a map, or direct communication?

10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.english: Deep Learning Theory (Double Descent and Grokking)
10_interdisciplinary_complexity_science_.text: Deep Learning Theory (Double Descent and Grokking): Classical statistics says: make your model too complex and it will overfit (memorize noise instead of learning the pattern).
Network Epidemiology: Classical epidemiology assumes everyone mixes randomly -- any person is equally likely to encounter any other.

Agent-Based Modeling (Computational Foundations): Agent-based modeling is like building a virtual world where you program individual "agents" (people, cells, animals, firms) with simple rules and then watch what happens when they interact.
Causal Emergence and Effective Information: Does all causation ultimately happen at the level of atoms and molecules?
Foundation Models for Scientific Discovery: AI is not just analyzing data -- it is making scientific discoveries.

AI for Scientific Discovery (Automated Science): When two brain regions both carry information about a stimulus, are they saying the same thing (redundancy), different things (unique information), or something that only emerges when you consider both together (synergy)?

10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.text: Sequence Alignment and Dynamic Programming: To compare two biological sequences (e.g., two genes from different species), we line them up to find the best match, allowing for insertions and deletions (gaps).
Phylogenetic Inference: By comparing DNA or protein sequences, we can reconstruct the evolutionary tree of life -- which species are most closely related and when they diverged.
Genome Assembly: Sequencing a genome is like shredding a book into small fragments and trying to reconstruct it.
Network Biology: Biology is organized as networks: genes regulate other genes, proteins interact with other proteins, metabolites are connected by chemical reactions.

Molecular Dynamics Simulation: Molecular dynamics is a computational microscope: it simulates the motion of every atom in a protein, DNA strand, or cell membrane, solving Newton's equations step by step.

10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.english: Hidden Markov Models in Biological Sequence Analysis
10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.text: Hidden Markov Models in Biological Sequence Analysis: Many biological sequences (DNA, proteins) have hidden structure: different regions of a gene serve different functions (exons, introns, regulatory regions), and the boundaries are not directly visible.
Protein Structure Prediction: Given just the sequence of amino acids, can we predict how the protein folds?
Gene Regulatory Network Inference: Genes don't work in isolation — they turn each other on and off in complex networks.

10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.english: Population Genetics Simulation and Coalescent Theory
10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.text: Population Genetics Simulation and Coalescent Theory: Instead of simulating evolution forward from the past, the coalescent looks backward from the present: when did our genes last share a common ancestor?

Machine Learning in Biology: Modern computational biology increasingly relies on machine learning to find patterns in massive biological datasets — from predicting the effect of mutations to classifying cell types from single-cell data to designing new proteins.
Systems Biology and Multi-Scale Modeling: Biology doesn't work at just one level — molecules affect cells, cells affect tissues, tissues affect organisms.
Single-Cell Genomics and Spatial Transcriptomics: Instead of grinding up tissue and measuring the average gene expression, single-cell genomics measures every individual cell separately.

10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.english: BLAST Algorithm and Heuristic Sequence Search
10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.text: BLAST Algorithm and Heuristic Sequence Search: BLAST is the most-used tool in bioinformatics.
Metabolic Flux Analysis: Metabolism is a vast network of chemical reactions.
Molecular Docking and Virtual Screening: Molecular docking is like trying every possible key in a lock -- computationally.
Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS): GWAS is a brute-force approach to finding which genetic variants are associated with a disease or trait.
RNA-Seq and Transcriptomic Analysis: RNA-seq tells you which genes are active (being read) in a cell or tissue, and how much.
Gillespie's Stochastic Simulation Algorithm: Inside a cell, chemical reactions do not happen continuously -- they happen as discrete, random events.
Metagenomics and Microbiome Analysis: Most microbes cannot be grown in the lab, so how do we study them?

10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.english: Variant Calling and Genomic Variant Analysis
10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.text: Variant Calling and Genomic Variant Analysis: After sequencing a person's genome, how do you find the places where their DNA differs from the reference?
Multi-Omics Data Integration: Measuring just one type of molecule (e.g., mRNA) gives an incomplete picture of a cell's state.
CRISPR Screen Analysis: CRISPR screens let you knock out every gene in the genome, one at a time, across millions of cells in a single experiment.

Foundation Models in Biology (ESM, AlphaFold): Just as GPT learned language by reading billions of words, biological foundation models learn the "language" of biology by processing millions of protein sequences, gene expression profiles, or molecular structures.

Spatial Transcriptomics Analysis: Standard single-cell RNA sequencing tells you what each cell is doing genetically, but it destroys the tissue -- you lose the information about where each cell was located.

Digital Twins in Medicine: Imagine a computer model of your own body -- a "digital twin" -- that knows your genome, your medical history, and your current health status, updated in real time from your wearable devices.
Causal Inference in Genomics (Mendelian Randomization): Does high cholesterol actually cause heart disease, or do they just happen to correlate?

10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.english: Foundation Models in Biology (Protein and Genomic Language Models)
10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.text: Foundation Models in Biology (Protein and Genomic Language Models): Just as ChatGPT learned language by reading billions of sentences, protein language models learn the "language" of proteins by reading billions of protein sequences.

10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.english: Spatial Transcriptomics and Computational Tissue Biology
10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.text: Spatial Transcriptomics and Computational Tissue Biology: Traditional genomics grinds up a tissue sample and measures the average gene expression, destroying all information about where each cell was.
Protein Design and RFdiffusion: For decades, scientists dreamed of designing proteins from scratch -- custom-made molecular machines that do not exist in nature.

10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.english: Evolutionary Scale Models and Fitness Prediction
10_interdisciplinary_computational_biolo.text: Evolutionary Scale Models and Fitness Prediction: Just as large language models like GPT learn the rules of human language by reading billions of sentences, protein language models learn the "language" of proteins by reading billions of amino acid sequences shaped by evolution.

10_interdisciplinary_data_science_princi.text: Bias-Variance Tradeoff: When building a predictive model, you face a fundamental tension: a simple model may be systematically wrong (bias), while a complex model may be too sensitive to the specific training data (variance).
The Curse of Dimensionality: In high dimensions, everything is far from everything else, and the amount of data you need to fill the space grows exponentially with the number of features.
Cross-Validation: How do you know if your model will work on new data?
Regularization: To prevent a model from memorizing the training data (overfitting), we add a penalty for complexity.
Statistical Learning Theory (VC Dimension): How complex can a model class be before it starts memorizing data instead of learning patterns?
The Bootstrap and Resampling Methods: How do you estimate the uncertainty of a statistical estimate when you have only one dataset?

10_interdisciplinary_data_science_princi.english: Causal Inference and the Distinction from Correlation
10_interdisciplinary_data_science_princi.text: Causal Inference and the Distinction from Correlation: Just because two things are correlated does not mean one causes the other.
Dimensionality Reduction and Manifold Learning: Data that looks high-dimensional often has a simpler underlying structure.
Ensemble Methods: Instead of relying on one model, train many models and combine their predictions.
Feature Engineering and Selection: How you represent your data to the algorithm is often more important than which algorithm you use.
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): Before building any model, look at your data.
Deep Learning Fundamentals: Deep learning stacks many simple processing layers to learn complex patterns directly from raw data.
The Manifold Hypothesis: Why does machine learning work so well on images, language, and other seemingly high-dimensional data?

Transfer Learning and Foundation Models: Instead of training a model from scratch for every new task, start with a model that has already learned general patterns from a large dataset and adapt it to your specific problem.
Fairness, Accountability, and Ethical AI: Algorithms can be unfair.

10_interdisciplinary_data_science_theore.text: No Free Lunch Theorem: There is no universally best learning algorithm.

10_interdisciplinary_data_science_princi.text: Information Criteria (AIC and BIC): When choosing between models, you want the one that best fits the data -- but a more complex model always fits better, even if the extra complexity is just fitting noise.
Bayesian Optimization: Bayesian optimization is a smart way to find the best settings for something when each experiment is expensive.
Simpson's Paradox: A treatment can look beneficial in every subgroup but harmful overall -- or vice versa.
Survival Analysis: How do you analyze data where the outcome is "time until something happens" and you do not always get to see the event?
Time Series Decomposition and Forecasting: Time series data is everywhere: stock prices, weather, web traffic, sensor readings.
Conformal Prediction: How confident should you be in a prediction?
Data Drift and Model Monitoring: A model trained on last year's data may fail on today's data because the world has changed.
Synthetic Data Generation: What if you need data to train a model but cannot use real data because it contains sensitive patient information, or you simply do not have enough?
Causal Machine Learning: Standard machine learning predicts what will happen.

10_interdisciplinary_data_science_princi.english: Conformal Prediction and Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification
10_interdisciplinary_data_science_princi.text: Conformal Prediction and Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification: Most machine learning models give you a prediction but no honest measure of how uncertain that prediction is.

10_interdisciplinary_data_science_princi.english: Foundation Models and Transfer Learning in Data Science
10_interdisciplinary_data_science_princi.text: Foundation Models and Transfer Learning in Data Science: Foundation models like GPT-4 and Claude represent a paradigm shift in data science: instead of building a specialized model for each task, you pre-train one massive model on enormous amounts of data, and then adapt it to whatever you need.
LLM-Powered Analytics and Data Agents: What if you could analyze data just by asking questions in plain English?
Data Mesh Architecture: What if you could create artificial data that looks, acts, and works just like real data — but does not contain any real person's information?

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: The Nature of Information: What is information?
Landauer's Principle: Erasing information has a physical cost: it must generate heat.
It from Bit (Wheeler): Is the universe ultimately made of information rather than matter and energy?

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: Information as a Fundamental Physical Quantity
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: Information as a Fundamental Physical Quantity: Information is not just an abstract concept -- it is a physical quantity with real constraints.

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: Semantic Information and the Challenge of Meaning
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: Semantic Information and the Challenge of Meaning: Shannon's theory tells us how much information a message carries, but not what it means.

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: Algorithmic Information Theory and Kolmogorov Complexity
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: Algorithmic Information Theory and Kolmogorov Complexity: How complex is a piece of data?
Levels of Abstraction (LoA): How you observe a system determines what you see.
The Ethics of Information (Information Ethics): Information has moral value.

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: Digital Physics and Computational Universe Hypothesis
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: Digital Physics and Computational Universe Hypothesis: What if the universe is literally a computer?

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: Information Closure and the Chinese Room
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: Information Closure and the Chinese Room: Even if a computer perfectly simulates understanding Chinese, does it actually understand?
Informational Structural Realism: At the deepest level, reality is not made of "stuff" (matter) but of patterns and relationships — information structures.

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: The Philosophy of AI and Machine Ethics
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: The Philosophy of AI and Machine Ethics: As AI becomes more capable, we face profound philosophical questions: Is AI truly intelligent?

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: Shannon vs. Semantic Information — The Fundamental Divide
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: Shannon vs. Semantic Information — The Fundamental Divide: Shannon's information theory counts bits but ignores meaning.
Information Closure: Some systems are informationally "self-contained" -- their future behavior is mostly determined by their own internal state, not by what is happening outside them.
Digital Ontology: Is the universe fundamentally digital (made of discrete bits) or analog (made of continuous quantities)?

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: Floridi's Method of Levels of Abstraction — Extended
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: Floridi's Method of Levels of Abstraction — Extended: Floridi's method of Levels of Abstraction is a powerful tool for thinking clearly about complex systems.
The Fourth Revolution (Floridi): Copernicus showed we are not at the center of the universe.

Constructor Theory (Deutsch and Marletto): Instead of asking "what happens given these initial conditions and these laws of motion?" constructor theory asks "what transformations are possible and what are impossible, and what must be true for them to be possible?" This is a radical shift.
Quantum Information Philosophy: Quantum information is fundamentally different from classical information.

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: The Logic of Information Flow (Barwise and Seligman)
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: The Logic of Information Flow (Barwise and Seligman): How does information flow from one system to another?
Info-Computational Naturalism (Dodig-Crnkovic): What if everything in nature -- from atomic interactions to brain processes -- is computation?
Philosophy of Computer Simulation: When a climate scientist runs a simulation of the Earth's atmosphere, or a biologist simulates protein folding, are the results real knowledge?
Information Thermodynamics: Information and energy are deeply connected.
Philosophy of Big Data: Big data promised to solve everything: with enough data, patterns reveal themselves without the need for theory.

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: Right to Be Forgotten and Information Fiduciary
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: Right to Be Forgotten and Information Fiduciary: Should you have the right to make embarrassing information about yourself disappear from the internet?

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: AI Existential Risk and the Alignment Problem
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: AI Existential Risk and the Alignment Problem: What happens when we build AI smarter than any human?

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: Philosophy of Simulation and Digital Ontology
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: Philosophy of Simulation and Digital Ontology: Computer simulations have become indispensable in science: we simulate the climate to predict global warming, simulate galaxies to test cosmological theories, and simulate economies to evaluate policies.

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: Epistemic Justice in the Information Age
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: Epistemic Justice in the Information Age: Who gets to know, and whose knowledge counts?

10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.english: AI Safety Philosophy and Existential Risk
10_interdisciplinary_philosophy_of_infor.text: AI Safety Philosophy and Existential Risk: How do we make sure that super-intelligent AI does not accidentally (or deliberately) cause a catastrophe?

Digital Personhood and Informational Identity: When a weather model predicts a hurricane, when AlphaFold predicts a protein structure, when a neural network diagnoses cancer from a medical image — do these algorithms "know" something?

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Lift Generation and Airfoil Theory: An airplane wing generates lift because air flowing over the curved upper surface speeds up and its pressure drops, while air below moves more slowly at higher pressure.
Bernoulli's Principle in Aerodynamics: In flowing air, where the velocity increases, the pressure decreases, and vice versa.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_the.text: Thin Airfoil Theory: Thin airfoil theory treats the wing cross-section as a nearly flat plate with some curvature (camber).
Prandtl Lifting-Line Theory: A real wing has finite span, so air leaks around the wingtips from high pressure below to low pressure above, creating trailing vortices.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: Compressible Flow and Mach Number Regimes
11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Compressible Flow and Mach Number Regimes: At low speeds, air acts like an incompressible fluid.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_law.text: Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation: A rocket works by throwing mass (exhaust) backward very fast, which pushes the rocket forward.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Specific Impulse: Specific impulse tells you how many seconds one kilogram of propellant can produce one kilogram of thrust.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_law.text: Kepler's Laws and Orbital Mechanics: Satellites and planets follow paths shaped by gravity.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Spacecraft Attitude Control: A spacecraft must point its antenna at Earth, its solar panels at the Sun, and its instruments at the target --- simultaneously and precisely.
Re-entry Aerothermodynamics: When a spacecraft comes back from orbit, it is traveling at ~28,000 km/h.
Structural Weight Optimization: Every kilogram of structure is a kilogram less of fuel or payload.
Aeroelasticity and Flutter: An aircraft wing is not rigid --- it bends and twists under air loads.
Radar Cross-Section and Stealth: Radar detects aircraft by sending out radio waves and listening for the echo.

Satellite Communication Link Budget: A satellite communication system must transmit a signal strong enough to be received clearly after traveling tens of thousands of kilometers through space and atmosphere.
Composite Materials in Aerospace: Carbon fiber composites are lighter and stronger than aluminum, which is why modern aircraft (Boeing 787, Airbus A350) are more than 50% composite by weight.
Orbital Perturbations and Station-Keeping: Kepler's laws describe a perfect world with a point-mass Earth.
Spacecraft Thermal Control: In space, a satellite can be broiling in sunlight on one side and freezing in shadow on the other.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: Composite Failure Modes in Aerospace Structures
11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Composite Failure Modes in Aerospace Structures: Unlike metals, which fail by yielding and cracking in a relatively straightforward way, composites can fail in many different ways: fibers can break, the resin matrix can crack, and the layers can peel apart (delaminate).

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: Aircraft Flight Dynamics and Stability Derivatives
11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Aircraft Flight Dynamics and Stability Derivatives: When an aircraft is disturbed from steady flight by a gust or a control input, it responds by pitching, rolling, and yawing.
Turbofan Propulsion Cycle Analysis: A turbofan engine produces thrust by accelerating a large mass of air by a small amount (the bypass stream) rather than a small mass by a large amount (pure jet).
Hypersonic Flow Phenomena: At hypersonic speeds --- five times the speed of sound and above --- air stops behaving like a simple gas.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: Space Debris and Orbital Environment Management
