11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Space Debris and Orbital Environment Management: Earth orbit is increasingly congested with space junk --- dead satellites, rocket parts, and millions of tiny fragments from collisions and explosions.

Mission Design and Delta-V Budgeting: Before building a spacecraft, engineers must design the mission trajectory and add up all the velocity changes (delta-v) needed to get from launch to the final orbit or destination.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: Digital Twin for Aerospace Vehicle Lifecycle Management
11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Digital Twin for Aerospace Vehicle Lifecycle Management: An aerospace digital twin is a virtual copy of a specific airplane or satellite that ages alongside the real vehicle.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: Additive Manufacturing Physics for Aerospace Structures
11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Additive Manufacturing Physics for Aerospace Structures: Aerospace 3D printing (additive manufacturing) builds metal parts by melting thin layers of metal powder with a laser.

Metamaterial and Morphing Aerodynamic Structures: Morphing wings change shape smoothly during flight --- twisting, bending, or changing their camber --- instead of using rigid flaps and slats with gaps.

Hypersonic Aerothermodynamics and Real-Gas Effects: When a vehicle flies faster than five times the speed of sound, the air in front of it gets so hot that it starts to change chemically --- oxygen molecules break apart, nitrogen molecules break apart, and the gas can even become electrically charged plasma.
Scramjet Propulsion Physics: A scramjet is a jet engine with no moving parts that works only at hypersonic speeds.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: Space Situational Awareness and Conjunction Assessment
11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Space Situational Awareness and Conjunction Assessment: With over 30,000 tracked objects in orbit (and millions of smaller debris pieces), space is increasingly congested.

fp_11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_.text: Electric Propulsion: Chemical rockets are like sprinters: huge thrust for short bursts.
Systems Engineering V-Model: Building an aircraft or spacecraft is too complex for any one person or team to grasp entirely.

fp_11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_.english: Boundary Layer Transition and Its Aerodynamic Effects
fp_11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_.text: Boundary Layer Transition and Its Aerodynamic Effects: When air flows over an aircraft wing, the thin layer of air nearest the surface (the boundary layer) starts out smooth and orderly (laminar).

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: Radiation Shielding for Deep-Space Human Missions
11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Radiation Shielding for Deep-Space Human Missions: Beyond Earth's protective magnetic field, astronauts are exposed to a constant barrage of high-energy particles from the galaxy and occasional intense bursts from the Sun.

Plasma Propulsion: Hall-Effect Thruster Physics: A Hall-effect thruster is an electric rocket engine that uses magnetic fields to trap electrons and electric fields to shoot ions out the back at enormous speed — 10 to 20 times faster than a chemical rocket's exhaust.
Aeroelastic Tailoring of Composite Wings: Composite materials let aerospace engineers do something impossible with metal wings: make a wing that automatically twists to unload itself when it bends.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: In-Situ Resource Utilization (ISRU) for Space Missions
11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: In-Situ Resource Utilization (ISRU) for Space Missions: ISRU means "living off the land" in space — using the materials available at your destination instead of bringing everything from Earth.

Computational Aeroacoustics and Noise Prediction: Computational aeroacoustics predicts the noise generated when air flows around and through aerospace vehicles and their components — jet engines, propellers, landing gear, airframe cavities, and helicopter rotors.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: Active Flow Control for Aerodynamic Performance
11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Active Flow Control for Aerodynamic Performance: Active flow control uses small devices on an aircraft surface to inject tiny amounts of energy into the airflow at precisely the right location and frequency to dramatically change how the air behaves.

11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.english: Cislunar Trajectory Design and Three-Body Dynamics
11_engineering_aerospace_engineering_pri.text: Cislunar Trajectory Design and Three-Body Dynamics: When navigating between the Earth and Moon, the simple two-body orbit equations used for Earth-centered missions break down because both Earth's and Moon's gravity matter simultaneously.

Rotating Detonation Engine Thermodynamics: A rotating detonation engine replaces the steady, gentle burning in a conventional jet engine combustion chamber with a continuously spinning detonation wave --- an explosion front traveling at supersonic speed around a ring-shaped chamber thousands of times per second.

Morphing Aircraft Structures: Morphing aircraft can smoothly change their wing shape in flight --- extending or folding the wingspan, curving the wing profile, or twisting the wing --- to be optimally efficient in every flight phase.

11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.text: Biocompatibility: When you put something in or on the human body, the body reacts.
Biomechanics: Tissue Mechanics: The body's tissues are remarkable engineering materials, but they behave nothing like steel or aluminum.

11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.english: Medical Imaging: X-ray and Computed Tomography
11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.text: Medical Imaging: X-ray and Computed Tomography: X-rays pass through the body, and different tissues absorb different amounts: bone absorbs a lot (appears white), air absorbs very little (appears black), and soft tissues are in between.
Medical Imaging: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI): MRI uses a powerful magnet and radio waves to make images of the body's soft tissues with exquisite detail and no radiation.
Medical Imaging: Ultrasound: Ultrasound works like sonar: it sends sound pulses into the body and listens for echoes that bounce back from tissue boundaries.
Medical Imaging: Positron Emission Tomography (PET): PET imaging traces the biological activity inside the body.

Biosensor Design: A biosensor is a device that detects a specific biological or chemical substance by combining a biological recognition element (like an enzyme that only reacts with glucose) with a physical detector (like an electrode that measures current).
Drug Delivery Systems: A pill dissolves and floods your body with drug all at once --- the level spikes (possibly causing side effects) and then drops (possibly becoming ineffective).
Tissue Engineering and Scaffolds: Tissue engineering aims to grow replacement body parts in the lab.
Prosthetics and Implant Design: When a joint, limb, or organ is damaged beyond repair, an engineered replacement must take over its function.

11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.english: Physiological Signal Processing: ECG and EEG
11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.text: Physiological Signal Processing: ECG and EEG: The heart and brain produce tiny electrical signals that can be picked up by electrodes on the skin.
Compartment Models in Pharmacokinetics: After you take a drug, it distributes throughout the body and is gradually eliminated.
Rehabilitation Engineering: Rehabilitation engineering helps people with disabilities regain independence.
Regulatory Principles: FDA Device Classification: Before any medical device can be sold, it must be shown to be safe and effective.

Neural Interfaces and Brain-Computer Interfaces: Neural interfaces are devices that connect directly to the brain or nerves, either to listen to neural signals (for controlling a computer or prosthetic) or to inject electrical stimulation (for treating Parkinson's disease, restoring hearing in cochlear implants, or restoring vision).

Bioinstrumentation: Amplification and Noise: Biological signals are tiny --- microvolts from the brain, millivolts from the heart --- and they must be extracted from a body that acts as an antenna for 50/60 Hz power-line noise.
Clinical Trials for Medical Devices: Before a new medical device can be marketed for high-risk applications, it must be tested in patients.
Microfluidics for Diagnostics: Microfluidics shrinks a laboratory onto a chip the size of a credit card.
3D Bioprinting: 3D bioprinting uses modified 3D printers to build living tissue structures layer by layer, using a "bioink" made of cells suspended in a gel-like material.

11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.english: Wearable Sensor Systems for Health Monitoring
11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.text: Wearable Sensor Systems for Health Monitoring: Wearable health devices --- smartwatches, fitness bands, patches, and smart clothing --- use tiny sensors to continuously track vital signs and activity.
Radiation Therapy Physics: Radiation therapy aims to destroy cancer cells while sparing healthy tissue.
Medical Robotics: Surgical robots do not operate autonomously --- they are sophisticated tools that extend the surgeon's capabilities.
Bioinformatics in Biomedical Engineering: Bioinformatics brings computing power to biology and medicine.
CRISPR-Based Diagnostic Platforms: CRISPR, famous for gene editing, has been repurposed as a powerful diagnostic tool.

Organ-on-a-Chip Microphysiological Systems: Organs-on-chips are thumbnail-sized devices containing living human cells arranged to mimic the structure and function of real organs --- lungs that breathe, hearts that beat, livers that metabolize drugs.

Digital Therapeutics: Software as Medicine: Digital therapeutics are prescription software programs --- typically smartphone apps --- that treat medical conditions the way drugs do, but through behavioral interventions, cognitive therapy, or physiological feedback instead of molecules.

Neuromorphic Brain-Computer Interfaces: A neuromorphic BCI reads electrical signals from brain neurons using tiny electrode arrays and decodes the person's intended movements or thoughts using a brain-inspired computer chip.

Piezoelectric Biomedical Energy Harvesting: Every time your heart beats, your lungs expand, or you take a step, mechanical energy is available that could be converted to electricity using piezoelectric materials.
Digital Biomarkers and Continuous Phenotyping: Digital biomarkers are health measurements gathered passively from the devices people already carry — smartphones and smartwatches.

fp_11_engineering_biomedical_engineering.text: Organ-on-a-Chip Technology: An organ-on-a-chip is a tiny device about the size of a USB drive that contains living human cells arranged to mimic how an actual organ works.

AI in Medical Image Analysis: AI can now analyze medical images --- X-rays, CT scans, pathology slides, eye photos --- with accuracy matching or exceeding that of specialist physicians for specific tasks.
Point-of-Care Diagnostics: Point-of-care testing brings the laboratory to the patient rather than sending samples to a distant lab.

11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.text: Tissue-on-Chip Platforms for Drug Screening: Tissue-on-chip platforms are miniature human body models on a microfluidic device — connecting tiny 3D tissue replicas of the liver, heart, kidney, and tumor through channels that mimic blood circulation.
Implantable Neural Interface Engineering: Implantable neural interfaces are tiny electrode arrays inserted into the brain or nerves to listen to neural signals and send electrical commands back.

11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.english: Bioprinting for Tissue and Organ Fabrication
11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.text: Bioprinting for Tissue and Organ Fabrication: Bioprinting is 3D printing with living cells instead of plastic.
Drug-Eluting Implant Engineering: Drug-eluting implants are medical devices coated with medication that slowly releases at the implant site.

11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.english: Photoacoustic Imaging for Deep Tissue Visualization
11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.text: Photoacoustic Imaging for Deep Tissue Visualization: Photoacoustic imaging is a hybrid technique that combines the best features of optical and ultrasound imaging.

Closed-Loop Neuromodulation Systems: Closed-loop neuromodulation is a smart brain stimulation system that listens to the brain, detects when something is going wrong, and delivers precisely the right amount of electrical stimulation to correct it — then backs off when the brain returns to normal.
Lab-on-a-Chip Diagnostic Microfluidics: A lab-on-a-chip is a miniature diagnostic laboratory built on a chip the size of a credit card.

Hydrogel Bioelectronics: Hydrogel bioelectronics replaces the hard, rigid materials of conventional electronics — silicon, metal, plastic — with soft, squishy, water-rich materials that feel like biological tissue.

11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.english: Acoustic Tweezing and Ultrasonic Particle Manipulation
11_engineering_biomedical_engineering_pr.text: Acoustic Tweezing and Ultrasonic Particle Manipulation: Acoustic tweezers use sound waves — ultrasound frequencies far above human hearing — to push, pull, sort, and trap tiny objects like cells, bacteria, and microparticles without any physical contact.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_law_.text: Conservation of Mass (Mass Balance): Matter is neither created nor destroyed (in non-nuclear processes).
Conservation of Energy (Energy Balance): Energy, like mass, is conserved.
Conservation of Momentum (Momentum Balance): Fluids flow because of pressure differences, gravity, and pumping.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.text: Unit Operations Concept: Whether you are making gasoline, antibiotics, or beer, you are doing the same basic things: heating, cooling, mixing, separating, and reacting.
Distillation: McCabe-Thiele Method: Distillation separates liquid mixtures by boiling --- the more volatile component concentrates in the vapor.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.english: Chemical Reactor Design: CSTR and PFR
11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.text: Chemical Reactor Design: CSTR and PFR: A CSTR is like a perfectly stirred pot --- the concentration inside is uniform and equals the outlet concentration.

Heat and Mass Transfer Analogies: Heat transfer and mass transfer obey the same mathematical structure: something (heat or molecules) diffuses down a gradient, and the rate depends on a driving force and a resistance.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_law_.english: Fick's Law of Diffusion in Engineering
11_engineering_chemical_engineering_law_.text: Fick's Law of Diffusion in Engineering: Molecules naturally spread from regions of high concentration to low concentration --- this is diffusion.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.text: Dimensionless Numbers in Chemical Engineering: Dimensionless numbers are ratios that tell you which physical effects dominate.
Process Control Fundamentals: Chemical processes are inherently dynamic --- temperatures drift, levels change, feed compositions vary.
Scale-Up Principles: What works in a flask may not work in a factory.
Catalytic Reactor Design: In a catalytic reactor, the reactants must travel from the flowing fluid to the catalyst surface, diffuse into the pores, react, and then the products must diffuse back out.
Separation Processes Overview: Mixing is spontaneous and easy; un-mixing requires energy and clever engineering.

Fluidization: If you blow air upward through sand fast enough, the sand particles lift off and the bed behaves like a boiling liquid --- particles circulate, mix vigorously, and the bed surface undulates.
Process Safety: HAZOP Methodology: Before starting up a chemical plant, engineers systematically ask "what if?" for every parameter at every point in the process.
Batch vs. Continuous Processing: Batch processing is like cooking a meal: you put ingredients in a pot, cook for a while, and take the product out.
Crystallization Engineering: Crystallization is how we make solid products with controlled purity and particle size --- from table sugar and pharmaceutical tablets to semiconductor-grade silicon.
Membrane Separation Processes: A membrane is like a very selective filter at the molecular level.
Gas Absorption and Stripping: Gas absorption is like dissolving carbon dioxide in water to make soda, but done on an industrial scale.
Process Optimization: A chemical process has many adjustable parameters: temperatures, pressures, flow rates, reflux ratios, reactor volumes.
Pinch Analysis for Heat Integration: In a chemical plant, some streams need to be heated and others need to be cooled.

Polymerization Reactor Design: Making a polymer is not like making a small molecule --- the product's properties (strength, flexibility, processability) depend critically on the chain length distribution and branching, which are controlled by reactor conditions.
Bioprocess Engineering: Bioprocess engineering uses living cells or enzymes as tiny chemical factories to make products: antibiotics, insulin, beer, biofuels, and monoclonal antibodies.
Safety Instrumented Systems: Process safety depends on layers of protection: good design, operator training, alarms, and --- as a last automated line of defense --- safety instrumented systems.
Environmental Engineering in Chemical Processes: Chemical processes inevitably produce waste --- contaminated water, exhaust gases, and solid residues.
Process Intensification: Microreactor Engineering: Microreactors shrink chemical processes from room-sized vessels to devices with tiny channels (thinner than a human hair).

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.english: Hydrogen Economy Thermodynamics and System Engineering
11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.text: Hydrogen Economy Thermodynamics and System Engineering: Hydrogen can be a carbon-free energy carrier, but thermodynamics sets hard limits at every step.
Carbon Capture Process Engineering: Carbon capture takes CO2 out of power plant or factory exhaust.
Process Analytical Technology (PAT): PAT means measuring product quality while it is being made, not after.
Microreactor and Flow Chemistry Engineering: Microreactors are tiny chemical factories where reactions happen inside channels thinner than a human hair.
Reactive Distillation Integration: Reactive distillation is a clever process that runs a chemical reaction and separates the products simultaneously in the same equipment.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_law_.text: Henry's Law for Gas-Liquid Equilibrium: The amount of gas that dissolves in a liquid is proportional to the gas pressure above it.

fp_11_engineering_chemical_engineering_p.text: Process Intensification: Process intensification asks: "What if we could make chemical equipment 10 or 100 times smaller while producing the same output?" The answer involves creating conditions where mixing and heat transfer happen so fast that the chemistry, not the transport, is the limiting step.
Electrochemical Reactor Engineering: Electrochemical engineering uses electricity to drive chemical reactions at electrodes.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.text: Membrane Separation Processes: Membrane separation works like a very precise filter — but at the molecular level.

Plasma-Assisted Combustion and Processing: Plasma-assisted combustion uses electrical discharges — like controlled lightning — to create highly reactive atoms and molecules in a gas before or during combustion.

Adsorption-Based Separation Processes: Adsorption separation works by passing a gas or liquid mixture through a bed of solid material that preferentially grabs certain molecules and lets others pass through.

Supercritical Fluid Processing: Supercritical fluid processing uses substances heated and pressurized beyond their critical point — a special state where they behave as both liquid and gas simultaneously.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.english: Process Digital Twin and Real-Time Optimization
11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.text: Process Digital Twin and Real-Time Optimization: A process digital twin is a virtual copy of a chemical plant that runs on a computer in perfect synchronization with the real plant.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.english: Green Chemistry Principles in Process Design
11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.text: Green Chemistry Principles in Process Design: Green chemistry redesigns chemical processes from the ground up to prevent pollution rather than cleaning it up afterward.
Electrochemical Membrane Reactor Engineering: Electrochemical membrane reactors combine two processes -- chemical reaction and separation -- into one device.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.english: Microreactor and Flow Chemistry Process Intensification
11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.text: Microreactor and Flow Chemistry Process Intensification: Microreactors are tiny continuous-flow chemical reactors where reactions happen in channels thinner than a human hair.

11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.english: CO2 Capture and Utilization Process Design
11_engineering_chemical_engineering_prin.text: CO2 Capture and Utilization Process Design: Carbon capture and utilization tackles the challenge of removing CO2 from power plant exhaust or even from the open atmosphere and turning it into something useful.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_la.text: Ohm's Law: Electrical resistance is like friction for current.
Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL): Electric charge does not pile up at a junction --- what flows in must flow out.
Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL): If you walk around any closed path in a circuit, adding up voltage rises and drops, you end up right where you started --- at the same potential.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_th.text: Thevenin's and Norton's Equivalents: No matter how complicated a circuit is inside a black box, from the outside (two terminals) it behaves exactly like a simple source with a single impedance.
Maximum Power Transfer: To squeeze the most power out of a source into a load, you match the load impedance to the conjugate of the source impedance.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.text: Impedance Matching: Imagine shouting into a pipe that suddenly changes diameter --- some sound bounces back.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_th.text: Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem: To capture all the information in a signal digitally, you must sample it at least twice as fast as its highest frequency component.
Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity: Every communication channel has a speed limit determined by how wide the frequency band is (bandwidth) and how strong the signal is relative to the noise.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.text: Feedback Control Theory (Negative Feedback): Feedback means measuring the output and using the difference between what you want and what you get (the error) to adjust the input.
Bode Stability Criterion: A feedback system becomes unstable when the signal fed back arrives with the wrong timing (phase) and sufficient strength (gain) to reinforce rather than correct the error.
PID Control: PID control is the workhorse of industrial automation.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_la.text: Semiconductor Physics: The p-n Junction: When p-type (excess holes) and n-type (excess electrons) semiconductors are joined, carriers near the junction diffuse across and recombine, creating a charge-depleted zone that acts as a one-way valve for current.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.text: MOSFET Operation: A MOSFET is a voltage-controlled switch.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_ax.text: Boolean Algebra in Digital Circuits: All digital computation reduces to combining true/false (1/0) values with simple logical operations.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.text: Power Electronics Fundamentals: Power electronics is the art of reshaping electrical power --- changing voltage levels, converting between AC and DC, and controlling motor speeds --- using fast-switching semiconductor devices.
Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC): Every electronic device is both a potential source of electrical noise and a potential victim of it.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.english: Signal Integrity in High-Speed Digital Design
11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.text: Signal Integrity in High-Speed Digital Design: At low speeds, a wire is just a wire.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_la.text: Transmission Line Theory: A transmission line (coaxial cable, PCB trace, waveguide) carries signals as waves traveling in both directions.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.text: Power System Load Flow Analysis: A power grid has generators, transmission lines, transformers, and loads spread across a wide area.
Power System Transient Stability: All large generators on the power grid spin in synchronism at the same electrical frequency (50 or 60 Hz).
Root Locus Method: The root locus is a graphical tool that shows how the behavior of a feedback control system changes as you turn the gain knob up from zero.
State-Space Representation: State-space is a way of describing a system using a set of first-order differential equations instead of a single high-order transfer function.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.english: Discrete Fourier Transform and Digital Signal Processing
11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.text: Discrete Fourier Transform and Digital Signal Processing: Digital signal processing converts analog signals to numbers, manipulates those numbers mathematically, and (optionally) converts them back.
Pulse-Width Modulation for Power Conversion: Power electronics converts electrical power by switching transistors on and off very rapidly.
Antenna Radiation Fundamentals: An antenna is the interface between an electrical circuit and the electromagnetic wave world.

Wide-Bandgap Semiconductor Device Physics: GaN and SiC can handle much higher voltages and switch much faster than silicon because their wider bandgap makes them far more resistant to electrical breakdown.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.english: Digital Twin Principles for Electrical Power Systems
11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.text: Digital Twin Principles for Electrical Power Systems: A power grid digital twin is a living computer model of the entire electrical network that continuously ingests data from thousands of sensors across the grid.

Hydrogen Electrolysis and Power-to-Gas Thermodynamics: Splitting water into hydrogen fuel using electricity requires at least 1.23 volts per cell, but real electrolyzers need about 1.8--2.2 volts because of various energy losses.

fp_11_engineering_electrical_engineering.text: MEMS/NEMS Electromechanical Physics: MEMS and NEMS are tiny machines fabricated on silicon chips using the same techniques as computer chips.
Piezoelectric Energy Harvesting: Certain crystals and ceramics generate voltage when squeezed or bent --- this is the piezoelectric effect.
Neuromorphic Engineering: Neuromorphic chips compute like brains instead of like conventional computers.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_la.text: Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction: When a magnetic field through a loop of wire changes --- whether because the magnet moves, the loop moves, or the field strength varies --- a voltage is induced that drives current.

11_engineering_electrical_engineering_pr.text: CMOS Logic Design: CMOS is the technology behind virtually all modern digital chips --- from microprocessors to memory.
Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) Fundamentals: A PLL is a circuit that locks onto an input frequency and generates a clean, stable output at that frequency (or a multiple of it).

Wide-Bandgap Power Electronics (GaN and SiC): Silicon carbide and gallium nitride are semiconductors with wider bandgaps than silicon, which means they can handle higher voltages, switch faster, and operate at higher temperatures while wasting far less energy.
Thermoelectric Energy Conversion: Thermoelectric devices generate electricity from heat differences without any moving parts — no turbines, no pistons, no working fluids.
Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) Systems: Wireless power transfer sends electricity through the air without wires.
Superconducting Power Systems: Superconducting power systems use special materials that conduct electricity with absolutely zero resistance when cooled below a critical temperature.
Optical Fiber Communication Fundamentals: Optical fiber communication sends data as light pulses through hair-thin glass fibers.
Grid-Scale Energy Storage Systems: Grid-scale energy storage acts like a giant rechargeable battery for the power grid.

Gallium Nitride Power Device Physics: Gallium nitride is a semiconductor material that enables power electronic devices far superior to silicon -- switching faster, losing less energy, and operating at higher temperatures.

Piezoelectric Vibration Energy Harvesting: Piezoelectric energy harvesting captures the tiny vibrations present everywhere -- in bridges, machinery, buildings, and even human movement -- and converts them into usable electrical energy.
Quantum Sensing and Metrology: Quantum sensors use the strange behavior of quantum mechanics to make measurements far more precise than any conventional technology allows.

11_engineering_materials_science_princip.text: Crystal Structure and Symmetry: Most engineering metals and ceramics are crystalline --- their atoms are arranged in repeating three-dimensional patterns, like bricks in a wall extended in all directions.
Crystal Defects and Their Role: A perfect crystal exists only in textbooks.
Phase Diagrams: A phase diagram is a map that tells you what phases (solid, liquid, or different crystal structures) are stable at any given temperature and composition.

11_engineering_materials_science_law_ms0.text: Strengthening Mechanisms: There are four main ways to make a metal stronger, and they all work by making it harder for dislocations to move: (1) Cold working --- create more dislocations that tangle and block each other.

11_engineering_materials_science_law_ms0.english: Diffusion in Solids (Fick's Laws Applied to Materials)
11_engineering_materials_science_law_ms0.text: Diffusion in Solids (Fick's Laws Applied to Materials): Atoms in a solid are not frozen in place --- they vibrate and occasionally jump to neighboring sites, especially at high temperatures.

11_engineering_materials_science_princip.text: Corrosion and Oxidation: Metals corrode because they naturally want to return to their oxidized state (ores).
Polymer Structure-Property Relationships: Polymers (plastics, rubbers, fibers) are made of extremely long chain molecules.
Ceramic Processing and Properties: Ceramics --- from bricks and porcelain to advanced silicon carbide and alumina --- are hard, heat-resistant, and chemically stable, but they break without warning.
Composite Mechanics: Rule of Mixtures: A composite is a combination of two materials --- strong, stiff fibers embedded in a softer matrix (like rebar in concrete).
Semiconductor Materials: Semiconductors are materials whose electrical conductivity can be precisely controlled by adding tiny amounts of impurity atoms (doping).
Shape Memory Alloys: Imagine bending a metal wire and then warming it up, only to watch it spring back to its original shape.
Biomaterials: Biocompatibility and Design: When you put a material inside the human body (a hip implant, a heart valve, a dental filling), the body reacts to it.
Nanomaterials: Size-Dependent Properties: At the nanoscale, materials behave differently from their bulk counterparts.
Additive Manufacturing of Materials: 3D printing for metals and other engineering materials builds parts layer by layer using a laser or electron beam to melt powder or wire.
Materials Characterization Techniques: To understand and improve materials, you must be able to see and measure their structure at every scale.
The Processing-Structure-Property-Performance Paradigm: How you make a material (casting, forging, heat treating, 3D printing) controls what it looks like inside (grain size, phases, defects).

Thin Film Deposition and Epitaxy: Thin film deposition is how we coat surfaces with extremely thin layers of material --- from anti-reflective coatings on eyeglasses to the transistor layers in microchips.
Surface Engineering and Coatings: The surface of a part is where almost all the action happens: wear, corrosion, friction, and fatigue cracks all start at the surface.

Piezoelectric Materials and Electromechanical Coupling: Piezoelectric materials are the bridge between mechanical and electrical worlds: squeeze them and they produce a voltage; apply a voltage and they change shape.
High-Entropy Alloys: Traditional alloys are based on one dominant element with small additions (steel is mostly iron, brass is mostly copper).

Failure Analysis Methodology: When an engineering component fails --- a turbine blade fractures, a pipeline leaks, a bridge member cracks --- failure analysis is the forensic investigation that determines why.

Powder Metallurgy and Sintering: Powder metallurgy is an alternative to casting or machining: you start with fine metal or ceramic powder, press it into shape (like pressing sand into a sandcastle mold), and then heat it until the particles bond together (sintering).
Material Degradation and Lifetime Prediction: No engineering material lasts forever.

Metamaterials Design: Architecture Beyond Chemistry: Metamaterials get their extraordinary properties from their internal structure --- repeating patterns of struts, voids, or hinges --- rather than from what they are made of.

11_engineering_materials_science_princip.english: Computational Materials Discovery: High-Throughput and Machine Learning
11_engineering_materials_science_princip.text: Computational Materials Discovery: High-Throughput and Machine Learning: Instead of synthesizing thousands of materials one at a time in the lab, scientists now use computers to predict material properties from their atomic structure.
Self-Healing Materials: Autonomous Damage Repair: Self-healing materials can repair themselves after being damaged, like biological tissue.
High-Entropy Alloy Design Principles: Traditional alloys are based on one main element with small additions (steel is iron with a bit of carbon and chromium).

Topology-Optimized Architected Materials: Architected materials are materials whose properties come from their internal geometric structure — like how a honeycomb is much stiffer and lighter than a solid block of the same amount of wax.
Tribological Surface Engineering: Tribological surface engineering means modifying just the surface of a component to control how it interacts with other surfaces in contact.

fp_11_engineering_materials_science_prin.text: Metamaterials: Metamaterials derive their properties not from what they are made of but from how they are structured.

Computational Materials Design (DFT and CALPHAD): Instead of making hundreds of alloys in the lab and testing each one, computational materials design uses quantum mechanics and thermodynamic databases to predict which compositions and processing routes will produce the desired properties.
Self-Healing Materials: Imagine a material that can heal its own cracks, like skin healing a cut.

11_engineering_materials_science_princip.english: Auxetic Materials and Negative Poisson's Ratio Structures
11_engineering_materials_science_princip.text: Auxetic Materials and Negative Poisson's Ratio Structures: Most materials get thinner when you stretch them — pull a rubber band and it narrows in the middle.

Functionally Graded Materials (FGMs): Functionally graded materials smoothly transition from one material to another — like a component that is pure metal on one side and pure ceramic on the other, with a seamless blend in between.

Shape Memory Polymers and 4D Printing: Shape memory polymers are smart plastics that can be molded into a temporary shape and then snap back to their original form when triggered by heat, light, or moisture.
Biomimetic Materials Design: Biomimetic materials copy the remarkable structural designs that evolution has perfected over millions of years.

Electrospinning and Nanofiber Engineering: Electrospinning uses a strong electric field to pull a thin stream of polymer solution from a needle tip, stretching it into fibers a thousand times thinner than a human hair.

Thermoelectric Materials and Energy Conversion: Thermoelectric materials are special semiconductors that generate electricity directly from a temperature difference — no moving parts, no turbines, no fluids.

Gradient Materials by Additive Manufacturing: Additive manufacturing can build components that smoothly transition from one material to another within the same part — for example, stainless steel on one end grading into a nickel superalloy on the other, or titanium transitioning to copper.

Radiation-Tolerant Nanostructured Materials: When materials are bombarded by radiation (neutrons in nuclear reactors, cosmic rays in space), the energetic particles knock atoms out of their positions, creating damage that accumulates over time — the material swells, hardens, becomes brittle, and eventually fails.
Mechanoluminescent Stress-Sensing Materials: Mechanoluminescent materials glow when they are squeezed, bent, or stretched — the brighter the glow, the higher the stress.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_la.text: Generalized Hooke's Law (Stress-Strain-Temperature Relations): When you load a material and change its temperature simultaneously, the resulting deformation is the sum of the mechanical strain (from stress) and the thermal strain (from temperature change).
Thermal Expansion: Almost all materials expand when heated and contract when cooled.
Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction: Heat flows from hot to cold, and the rate of flow depends on how steep the temperature difference is and how well the material conducts heat.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Convective Heat Transfer: When a fluid (air, water, oil) flows over a warm surface, it carries heat away.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_la.text: Stefan-Boltzmann Law of Thermal Radiation: Every object radiates heat as electromagnetic waves.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.english: Heat Exchanger Analysis (LMTD and NTU Methods)
11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Heat Exchanger Analysis (LMTD and NTU Methods): A heat exchanger transfers heat between two fluids without mixing them.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.english: Carnot Cycle and Thermodynamic Cycle Efficiency
11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Carnot Cycle and Thermodynamic Cycle Efficiency: There is an absolute upper limit to how efficiently you can convert heat into work, set by the temperatures you are working between.
Rankine and Brayton Power Cycles: Steam power plants boil water, expand the steam through a turbine, condense it back to water, and pump it back to the boiler.
Refrigeration and Heat Pump Cycles: A refrigerator or air conditioner does not "create cold" --- it pumps heat from inside (cold side) to outside (hot side), requiring work input.
Euler Turbomachinery Equation: The power a turbine extracts or a compressor adds depends entirely on how much the fluid's swirl (tangential velocity) changes as it passes through the rotor.
Tribology: Friction, Wear, and Lubrication: Whenever two surfaces rub against each other, there is friction (which resists motion) and wear (which removes material).

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.english: Vibration Analysis: Natural Frequency and Damping
11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Vibration Analysis: Natural Frequency and Damping: Every structure and machine has natural frequencies at which it "wants" to vibrate.
Fatigue Analysis in Machine Design: Machine parts that are loaded repeatedly --- shafts, gears, springs, bolts --- can break even though no single load cycle is large enough to cause failure.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_th.text: Grashof's Law for Four-Bar Mechanisms: A four-bar linkage is the simplest mechanism that converts one type of motion into another (like a crank turning rotation into a rocking motion).

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Manufacturing Tolerances and Fits: Every part comes out slightly different from the blueprint.

Ashby Material Selection Methodology: Choosing the right material is not about picking the strongest or lightest --- it is about finding the best combination of properties for the specific function, objective (minimize weight?
Reliability Engineering (Bathtub Curve and MTBF): Everything eventually breaks.
Dimensional Analysis and Similitude: Before building a full-size bridge, airplane, or ship, engineers test scale models.
Psychrometrics and HVAC Design: Air is not just air --- it contains water vapor, and the amount of moisture profoundly affects human comfort and building energy use.
Computational Fluid Dynamics: CFD uses computers to simulate fluid flow by dividing the flow domain into millions of tiny cells and solving the equations of fluid motion in each cell.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.english: Finite Element Analysis for Mechanical Design
11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Finite Element Analysis for Mechanical Design: Finite element analysis (FEA) is the computer-based method mechanical engineers use to predict stresses, deformations, temperatures, and vibration modes in complex parts.
Additive Manufacturing for Mechanical Design: 3D printing has moved from prototyping to producing functional mechanical parts.
Mechatronics Integration: Most modern machines are mechatronic: they combine mechanical parts with electronics, sensors, and software.
Robotics: Forward and Inverse Kinematics: A robot arm has several rotating joints connected by links.
Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH) Engineering: Every car, appliance, and machine produces noise and vibration.
4D Printing: Shape-Memory Additive Manufacturing: 4D printing adds time as the fourth dimension to 3D printing.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.english: Digital Twin Framework for Mechanical Systems
11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Digital Twin Framework for Mechanical Systems: A digital twin for a machine --- an engine, turbine, or robot --- is a computer replica that stays synchronized with the real machine through continuous sensor data.
Process Intensification in Mechanical Design: Process intensification makes chemical and thermal equipment dramatically smaller and more efficient by redesigning it from first principles.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.english: Tribology Fundamentals: Friction, Wear, and Lubrication
11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Tribology Fundamentals: Friction, Wear, and Lubrication: Tribology is the science of surfaces rubbing together.
Piezoelectric Actuators and Precision Motion: Piezoelectric actuators are solid-state devices that expand or contract when voltage is applied, with no moving parts, no friction, and no backlash.
Topology-Optimized Conformal Cooling Channels: In injection molding and die casting, parts must be cooled inside the mold before removal.

fp_11_engineering_mechanical_engineering.text: Topology Optimization in Mechanical Design: Traditional design starts with a shape and checks if it works.
Digital Twin Concept: Imagine having a perfect computer model of a jet engine, a wind turbine, or a factory floor that updates itself in real time with sensor readings from the actual machine.

fp_11_engineering_mechanical_engineering.english: Shape Memory Alloy Actuators in Mechanical Systems
fp_11_engineering_mechanical_engineering.text: Shape Memory Alloy Actuators in Mechanical Systems: SMA actuators are solid-state devices that produce motion by changing temperature.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.english: Shape Memory Polymer Actuators and Multifunctional Structures
11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Shape Memory Polymer Actuators and Multifunctional Structures: Shape memory polymers are plastics that remember their original shape.
Microfluidic Heat Exchangers: Microfluidic heat exchangers cool things incredibly effectively by forcing liquid through channels thinner than a human hair.
Compliant Mechanisms for Precision Engineering: Compliant mechanisms are structures that move by bending rather than by using hinges and bearings.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.english: Soft Robotics Actuators and Continuum Mechanisms
11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Soft Robotics Actuators and Continuum Mechanisms: Soft robotics replaces the rigid metal arms and motors of traditional robots with squishy, inflatable structures made of silicone rubber.

Origami-Inspired Engineering and Deployable Mechanisms: Origami engineering takes the art of paper folding and applies it to create structures that can transform between a flat, compact state and a large, functional 3D shape.

11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.english: Digital Thread and Model-Based Systems Engineering
11_engineering_mechanical_engineering_pr.text: Digital Thread and Model-Based Systems Engineering: The digital thread is the idea that every piece of information about a product — from the first customer requirement through 3D design, stress analysis, manufacturing instructions, test results, and field service records — is connected in a single, continuous digital chain.
Biomechanics of Terrestrial Locomotion: The biomechanics of locomotion explains how humans and animals walk and run efficiently.
Vibration-Based Energy Harvesting Systems: Every vibrating surface is a potential energy source.
Rotating Detonation Engine Thermodynamics: A rotating detonation engine uses controlled explosions -- not the gentle burning of conventional jet engines -- to combust fuel far more efficiently.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_la.text: Hooke's Law for Structural Materials: When you pull or push on a material gently enough, it deforms in direct proportion to the applied force, and it springs back when you let go.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Equilibrium (Static): A structure that is not accelerating must have all its forces and twisting effects perfectly balanced.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_th.text: Euler Buckling (Column Stability): A long, thin column under compression will suddenly bow sideways at a specific load rather than simply shortening.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_la.text: Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory: When a beam bends, the top might compress while the bottom stretches (or vice versa).

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Mohr's Circle for Stress Transformation: Stress at a point changes depending on the orientation of the plane you examine.

Saint-Venant's Principle: If you apply the same total force and moment to a structure but distribute them differently over a small patch, the stress fields will differ near the patch but will look essentially identical far away.
Factor of Safety: Engineers deliberately design structures to be stronger than strictly necessary because materials vary, loads are uncertain, analysis is approximate, and construction is imperfect.
Load Path Continuity: Forces do not vanish --- they must travel through the structure.
Structural Redundancy and Indeterminacy: A structure is "redundant" when it has more supports or members than the bare minimum needed to stand.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_th.text: Plastic Hinge Theory (Limit Analysis): Steel and other ductile materials do not break the instant they start yielding --- they deform plastically.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Prestressing Principle: Concrete is strong in compression but weak in tension.

Finite Element Method in Structures: Instead of solving the full equations of elasticity for a complex shape, you break the structure into many small, simple pieces (triangles, rectangles, tetrahedra).

11_engineering_structural_engineering_la.english: Fatigue Life and S-N Curves (Wohler's Law)
11_engineering_structural_engineering_la.text: Fatigue Life and S-N Curves (Wohler's Law): Bending a paper clip back and forth will break it even though the force is too small to break it in one pull.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_th.text: Griffith Fracture Criterion: Materials contain tiny flaws.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Wind Loading on Structures: Wind exerts pressure on the windward face and suction on the leeward face and roof.
Seismic Design Philosophy: Buildings in earthquake zones are not designed to remain undamaged in every earthquake --- that would be prohibitively expensive.
Soil-Structure Interaction: A building does not sit on infinitely rigid ground --- the soil beneath it compresses, shifts, and absorbs energy.
Composite Material Behavior in Structures: Composites like carbon-fiber-reinforced polymer are strong in the fiber direction but weaker across it.
Connection Design Philosophy: The chain is only as strong as its weakest link, and in structures, that weak link is often a connection.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.english: Response Spectrum Method for Seismic Analysis
11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Response Spectrum Method for Seismic Analysis: Rather than simulating a building's full time-history response to an earthquake (computationally expensive), engineers use a response spectrum: a chart that shows the worst-case shaking experienced by structures of different natural periods.
Vortex-Induced Vibration and Lock-In: Wind flowing past a bridge cable, chimney, or tower creates alternating vortices that push the structure side to side.
Progressive Collapse Resistance: If one column in a building is destroyed --- by a bomb, a vehicle impact, or a gas explosion --- the structure should not collapse like a house of cards.

Performance-Based Structural Design: Traditional structural design simply asks: "Will the building collapse in a big earthquake?" Performance-based design asks much more: "In a small earthquake, will it remain fully operational?

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.english: Durability and Corrosion of Reinforced Concrete
11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Durability and Corrosion of Reinforced Concrete: Reinforced concrete is not permanent --- it degrades.
Structural Health Monitoring: Rather than waiting for cracks to become visible or a bridge to fail, engineers can instrument structures with sensors that continuously listen for signs of trouble.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.english: Digital Twin Methodology for Structural Systems
11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Digital Twin Methodology for Structural Systems: A digital twin is a living computer model of a real structure --- a bridge, building, or dam --- that updates itself using sensor measurements from the actual structure.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.english: Topology Optimization Convergence for Additive Manufacturing
11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Topology Optimization Convergence for Additive Manufacturing: Topology optimization uses computers to figure out the most efficient shape for a structural part by removing material wherever it is not needed.

Metamaterial-Based Seismic Cloaking: Seismic metamaterials are engineered arrays of buried structures --- concrete columns, steel-mass resonators, or even boreholes --- arranged around a building to deflect or absorb earthquake waves before they arrive.

fp_11_engineering_structural_engineering.text: Tensegrity Structural Systems: Tensegrity structures are built from bars that float within a web of cables, never touching each other.

Topology-Optimized Lattice Structures: Instead of using solid material everywhere, lattice structures fill a part with tiny repeating patterns --- like a honeycomb or a miniature truss network --- where the pattern can change from region to region.

Robustness and Fail-Safe Structural Design: A robust structure is one that does not collapse catastrophically when one part is damaged or removed --- like a net that still holds even when one strand is cut.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Topology Optimization for Structural Design: Topology optimization asks the computer: "Given this space, these loads, and this much material, where should material be placed for maximum structural efficiency?" The result often looks like bones or tree branches --- organic shapes where every piece of material is working hard.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.english: Creep and Relaxation in Concrete Structures
11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Creep and Relaxation in Concrete Structures: Concrete under sustained load keeps deforming slowly over months and years, even though no additional load is applied.

Capacity Design Hierarchy: Capacity design is like installing a fuse in an electrical circuit: you deliberately make certain elements the weakest link so that when overloaded, failure occurs there --- where you have designed it to be safe and ductile --- rather than in a brittle or critical location.
Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) Systems: Structural health monitoring is like giving a building, bridge, or aircraft a nervous system.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.english: Functionally Graded Materials in Structural Applications
11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Functionally Graded Materials in Structural Applications: Instead of bonding two different materials together with a sharp boundary — which creates stress concentrations that can cause cracking and delamination — functionally graded materials smoothly transition from one material to another across a distance.
Compliant Mechanism Design: A compliant mechanism is a structure that bends to create motion instead of using hinges and joints.
Structural Glass Engineering: Structural glass engineering goes beyond using glass as a window — it makes glass carry structural loads like beams, columns, and floors.
Cable-Stayed and Suspension Structure Mechanics: Cable-stayed and suspension structures use strong steel cables to hold up bridge decks and roofs over enormous spans.
Performance-Based Wind Engineering: Performance-based wind engineering goes beyond checking whether a building survives the design wind storm.

Fiber-Reinforced Polymer Composite Structural Systems: FRP composites combine strong fibers with plastic resin to create structural materials that are 4-5 times stronger than steel per unit weight and completely immune to corrosion.

11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.english: Prestressed Concrete Advanced Loss Estimation and Time-Dependent Analysis
11_engineering_structural_engineering_pr.text: Prestressed Concrete Advanced Loss Estimation and Time-Dependent Analysis: Prestressed concrete uses tensioned steel cables to compress the concrete before loading, preventing tensile cracks.

Origami-Based Metamaterial Structures: Origami metamaterials are engineered structures based on folding patterns that give them extraordinary mechanical behaviors impossible in conventional materials.

12_medical_sciences_clinical_medicine_pr.text: Precision Medicine and Pharmacogenomics: Precision medicine recognizes that patients are not all the same and that their genetic makeup affects how they respond to drugs.

Liquid Biopsy in Clinical Diagnosis: A liquid biopsy is a simple blood draw that can detect cancer, track how well treatment is working, and identify drug-resistance mutations --- all without an invasive tissue biopsy.

Digital Therapeutics and Prescription Software: Digital therapeutics are prescription smartphone apps that treat medical conditions through structured behavioral programs, just as drugs treat conditions through chemistry.
Gut-Brain Axis in Clinical Medicine: Your gut and brain are in constant conversation through nerves, hormones, immune signals, and chemicals made by gut bacteria.

12_medical_sciences_clinical_medicine_pr.english: Tumor Mutational Burden as a Biomarker
12_medical_sciences_clinical_medicine_pr.text: Tumor Mutational Burden as a Biomarker: Tumor mutational burden counts how many DNA mutations a cancer has.
Implementation Science in Clinical Practice: Implementation science is about closing the gap between what we know works in medicine and what actually happens in clinics and hospitals.

fp_12_medical_sciences_clinical_medicine.text: Precision Medicine: Precision medicine recognizes that every patient is different and aims to match the right treatment to the right patient at the right time.
Telemedicine Principles: Telemedicine lets doctors care for patients without being in the same room.
Antimicrobial Resistance and Clinical Stewardship: Bacteria, viruses, and fungi evolve resistance to the drugs used to kill them.

12_medical_sciences_clinical_medicine_pr.text: Polypharmacy and Deprescribing Principles: As people age and accumulate chronic conditions, they often end up taking many medications simultaneously — sometimes 10, 15, or more.

Chronotherapy and Circadian Medicine: Your body runs on a 24-hour internal clock that affects almost everything — when your blood pressure peaks, when your liver is most active at processing drugs, when your immune system is strongest, and when tumor cells divide fastest.

12_medical_sciences_clinical_medicine_pr.english: Circulating Tumor DNA as a Liquid Biopsy
12_medical_sciences_clinical_medicine_pr.text: Circulating Tumor DNA as a Liquid Biopsy: When cancer cells die, they release tiny fragments of their DNA into the bloodstream.

Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) in Clinical Assessment: Point-of-care ultrasound puts a portable ultrasound machine in the hands of the treating clinician — at the bedside, in the emergency department, or in the field — to answer specific clinical questions within minutes.

Shared Decision-Making and Patient-Centered Care: Shared decision-making recognizes that for many medical decisions, there is no single "right" answer — the best choice depends on what matters most to the patient.
Sepsis Pathophysiology and Protocolized Management: Sepsis occurs when the body's response to an infection spirals out of control, damaging its own organs.

Tumor Dormancy and Metastatic Latency: After cancer surgery removes the primary tumor, individual cancer cells may already have traveled to distant organs --- bone marrow, liver, lungs, brain --- where they can sit dormant for years or even decades before waking up and growing into metastases.

12_medical_sciences_clinical_medicine_pr.english: Digital Twin Modeling in Clinical Medicine
12_medical_sciences_clinical_medicine_pr.text: Digital Twin Modeling in Clinical Medicine: A clinical digital twin is a virtual replica of an individual patient --- built from their medical images, lab results, genetic data, and continuous monitoring --- that doctors can use to simulate different treatments before applying them to the real patient.

Psychedelic-Assisted Psychotherapy: Psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy is a carefully structured treatment combining psychedelic drugs with professional psychological support for conditions like treatment-resistant depression, PTSD, and addiction.

12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.text: Wastewater-Based Epidemiology: By testing a city's sewage, epidemiologists can estimate how many people are infected with a pathogen --- even those without symptoms who never get tested --- days before clinical cases appear.
Digital Epidemiology and Syndromic Surveillance 2.0: Digital epidemiology uses data from the internet, smartphones, and wearable devices to track disease outbreaks in near-real time.

12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.english: Target Trial Emulation for Observational Causal Inference
12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.text: Target Trial Emulation for Observational Causal Inference: Target trial emulation is a disciplined way to use observational data (hospital records, insurance claims) to answer cause-and-effect questions that would ideally be answered by a randomized trial.
Syndromic Surveillance Systems: Syndromic surveillance is an early warning system that detects disease outbreaks before doctors even diagnose them.

12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.english: Social Network Analysis in Disease Transmission
12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.text: Social Network Analysis in Disease Transmission: People do not interact randomly — they have specific networks of contacts at home, work, school, and social settings, and some people have far more contacts than others.
Negative Outcome Control Methods: Geospatial epidemiology maps where diseases occur and uses statistical methods to find patterns.
Syndemic Theory in Epidemiology: Syndemic theory recognizes that diseases do not occur in isolation — they cluster in vulnerable populations and make each other worse.

12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.english: Precision Public Health and Geospatially Targeted Interventions
12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.text: Precision Public Health and Geospatially Targeted Interventions: Traditional public health applies the same interventions broadly to entire populations — everyone gets the same screening recommendation, the same vaccination campaign, the same health message.
Mendelian Randomization in Causal Epidemiology: Mendelian randomization is a clever way to test whether something actually causes a disease, using genetics as a natural experiment.
Genomic Epidemiology and Phylodynamics: Genomic epidemiology uses the DNA sequences of pathogens to track how diseases spread — like using fingerprints to trace a crime.

Difference-in-Differences Causal Inference: Difference-in-differences is a method for estimating whether a policy or intervention actually caused a change in health outcomes when a randomized trial is impossible.

Agent-Based Modeling in Epidemic Simulation: Agent-based models simulate an epidemic by creating a virtual city (or country) populated by millions of virtual people, each with their own age, household, school or workplace, and daily routine.

12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.english: Interrupted Time Series Analysis for Policy Evaluation
12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.text: Interrupted Time Series Analysis for Policy Evaluation: Interrupted time series analysis is a method for evaluating whether a policy --- a new law, a public health campaign, a drug safety warning --- actually changed health outcomes.

12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.english: Multiplex Spatial Epidemiology and Syndemic Mapping
12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.text: Multiplex Spatial Epidemiology and Syndemic Mapping: Multiplex spatial epidemiology maps multiple diseases simultaneously across geography to find where they cluster together --- the neighborhoods and communities where diabetes AND depression AND substance use AND poverty all concentrate, reinforcing each other as a syndemic.

12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.english: Emulated Target Trials Using Electronic Health Records
12_medical_sciences_epidemiology_princip.text: Emulated Target Trials Using Electronic Health Records: Target trial emulation asks: if we could run the perfect randomized trial to answer this clinical question, what would it look like?

12_medical_sciences_immunology_principle.text: CAR-T Cell Engineering: Design Principles: CAR-T therapy takes a patient's own immune T cells, genetically reprograms them in the laboratory to recognize a specific marker on cancer cells, grows billions of these engineered cells, and infuses them back into the patient.
mRNA Vaccine Immunology: mRNA vaccines deliver genetic instructions (not the virus itself) wrapped in a tiny fat bubble.
Systems Immunology: Multi-Omics Network Analysis: Systems immunology studies the immune system as a whole, integrated network rather than examining individual components in isolation.

Trained Immunity (Innate Immune Memory): The traditional view was that only the adaptive immune system (T and B cells) has memory — that innate immune cells (like macrophages) respond identically every time.

12_medical_sciences_immunology_principle.english: Immune Exhaustion and T Cell Dysfunction
12_medical_sciences_immunology_principle.text: Immune Exhaustion and T Cell Dysfunction: When T cells fight a chronic infection (like HIV or hepatitis) or a cancer that they cannot clear, they become progressively exhausted — like a soldier who has been fighting for too long without rest.
Mucosal-Associated Invariant T (MAIT) Cells: Traditional immunology studies one cell type, one receptor, or one cytokine at a time.

Mucosal Immunology and Secretory IgA: The mucosal immune system guards the body's vulnerable interior surfaces — the gut, lungs, and reproductive tract — where we are most exposed to the outside world.
Trained Immunity: Innate Immune Memory: The textbook says only the adaptive immune system (T cells and B cells) has memory.

12_medical_sciences_immunology_principle.english: Immune Checkpoint Therapy: Beyond PD-1 and CTLA-4
12_medical_sciences_immunology_principle.text: Immune Checkpoint Therapy: Beyond PD-1 and CTLA-4: The first generation of immunotherapy drugs (anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4) release the brakes on T cells, enabling them to attack cancer.

12_medical_sciences_immunology_principle.english: Chimeric Antigen Receptor NK Cell Immunotherapy
12_medical_sciences_immunology_principle.text: Chimeric Antigen Receptor NK Cell Immunotherapy: CAR-NK cells are a next-generation cancer immunotherapy that takes natural killer cells — the immune system's innate assassins — and supercharges them with a synthetic targeting receptor.
Immune Metabolism Reprogramming in Disease: Immune cells dramatically rewire their internal metabolism when they are activated.

12_medical_sciences_immunology_principle.english: Bispecific Antibody Engineering for Immune Redirection
12_medical_sciences_immunology_principle.text: Bispecific Antibody Engineering for Immune Redirection: Bispecific antibodies are Y-shaped molecules where each arm grabs a different target.

Complement System Therapeutic Targeting: The complement system is a rapid-fire chemical cascade in the blood that punches holes in bacteria, coats them for immune cell consumption, and recruits inflammatory cells to infection sites.

Mucosal-Associated Invariant T Cell Biology: MAIT cells are a recently appreciated type of immune cell that represents a major component of human immunity --- making up 1-10% of all blood T cells and up to half of all T cells in the liver.

Tissue-Resident Memory T Cell Immunosurveillance: Tissue-resident memory T cells are immune sentinels permanently stationed in the body's barrier tissues --- skin, lungs, gut, reproductive tract --- where they stand guard long after an infection has resolved.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: Spatial Transcriptomics in Tissue Pathology: Spatial transcriptomics lets pathologists see which genes are active in every tiny region of a tissue sample, creating a detailed "molecular map" overlaid on the familiar microscope image.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.english: Liquid Biopsy Pathology: Circulating Tumor DNA and Beyond
12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: Liquid Biopsy Pathology: Circulating Tumor DNA and Beyond: Liquid biopsy examines tiny traces of cancer in a patient's blood --- fragments of tumor DNA, whole tumor cells, and small vesicles shed by tumors.

Artificial Intelligence in Diagnostic Pathology: AI pathology uses deep learning algorithms to analyze digital microscope images of tissue, performing tasks that traditionally require years of pathologist training.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.english: Tumor Mutational Burden and Neoantigen Pathology
12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: Tumor Mutational Burden and Neoantigen Pathology: Every cancer has DNA mutations, but some cancers have far more mutations than others.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.english: Ferroptosis and Regulated Non-Apoptotic Cell Death
12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: Ferroptosis and Regulated Non-Apoptotic Cell Death: Ferroptosis is a recently discovered way that cells die, driven by iron and oxidative damage to the fatty membranes of cells.
Spatial Transcriptomics in Tissue Pathology: Traditional pathology examines tissue as a mixture of many cell types — the result is an average that can hide what individual cells are doing.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.english: Digital Pathology and AI-Assisted Histopathological Diagnosis
12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: Digital Pathology and AI-Assisted Histopathological Diagnosis: Digital pathology replaces the traditional microscope with a computer screen: glass slides are scanned into extremely high-resolution digital images that pathologists can view, annotate, and share anywhere in the world.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.english: Liquid Biopsy Pathology: Circulating Tumor Cells and Cell-Free DNA
12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: Liquid Biopsy Pathology: Circulating Tumor Cells and Cell-Free DNA: A liquid biopsy is a blood test that detects cancer by finding tumor cells or tumor DNA fragments floating in the bloodstream.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.english: Ferroptosis as a Pathological Cell Death Mechanism
12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: Ferroptosis as a Pathological Cell Death Mechanism: Ferroptosis is a way cells die when iron-driven chemical reactions cause their membrane lipids to go rancid — essentially, the cell membrane oxidizes from the inside out, like metal rusting.
Clonal Hematopoiesis and Premalignant Evolution: As people age, some of their blood-forming stem cells acquire random DNA mutations that give them a growth advantage over neighboring cells.
Extracellular Vesicle Pathobiology: Extracellular vesicles are tiny bubbles released by cells that carry messages to other cells throughout the body.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.english: The Tumor Immune Microenvironment as Pathological Ecosystem
12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: The Tumor Immune Microenvironment as Pathological Ecosystem: The tumor immune microenvironment is the neighborhood surrounding a tumor — a complex mix of immune cells, blood vessels, connective tissue, and signaling molecules that collectively determine whether the immune system will attack or tolerate the cancer.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.english: Multiplex Spatial Proteomics in Tissue Pathology
12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: Multiplex Spatial Proteomics in Tissue Pathology: Multiplex spatial proteomics allows pathologists to simultaneously visualize 40-60 different proteins on a single tissue slide, painting a detailed molecular portrait of every cell in a tissue sample while preserving its spatial architecture.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.english: Tumor Dormancy Pathology and Disseminated Tumor Cell Niches
12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: Tumor Dormancy Pathology and Disseminated Tumor Cell Niches: When cancer is removed by surgery, microscopic cancer cells may already have seeded distant organs --- especially bone marrow.

12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.english: Senescence-Associated Secretory Phenotype in Disease Pathology
12_medical_sciences_pathology_principle_.text: Senescence-Associated Secretory Phenotype in Disease Pathology: Senescent cells are cells that have permanently stopped dividing but refuse to die.

12_medical_sciences_pharmacology_princip.text: PROTAC Targeted Protein Degradation: PROTACs are molecular "hitmen" that hijack the cell's own protein disposal system.
Antibody-Drug Conjugate Pharmacology: An antibody-drug conjugate is a guided missile: an antibody steers a potent toxin specifically to cancer cells.
Pharmacomicrobiomics: Gut Microbiome-Drug Interactions: The trillions of bacteria in your gut are a previously overlooked "organ" that processes drugs before and after your body absorbs them.

Pharmacovigilance Signal Detection: After a drug is approved and millions of people start taking it, rare side effects that were invisible in clinical trials (which test only thousands of patients) begin to emerge.
Pharmacomicrobiomics: The trillions of bacteria in your gut can activate, inactivate, or transform the medications you take, dramatically affecting whether a drug works or causes side effects.
RNA Therapeutics Pharmacology: Gene therapy treats disease by delivering a working copy of a gene directly into a patient's cells.
Chronopharmacology and Circadian Drug Dosing: Your body's internal clock affects how it handles every medication.

12_medical_sciences_pharmacology_princip.english: Microbiome Therapeutics and Fecal Microbiota Transplantation
12_medical_sciences_pharmacology_princip.text: Microbiome Therapeutics and Fecal Microbiota Transplantation: Fecal microbiota transplantation — essentially transplanting the gut bacteria from a healthy person into a sick person — is remarkably effective for treating recurrent C.

12_medical_sciences_pharmacology_princip.english: Allosteric Modulation and Bitopic Ligand Pharmacology
12_medical_sciences_pharmacology_princip.text: Allosteric Modulation and Bitopic Ligand Pharmacology: Most drugs work by fitting into the same binding site as the body's natural chemical messenger, directly blocking or activating the receptor.
Pharmacogenomic Dosing Algorithms: Pharmacogenomic dosing algorithms use a patient's DNA to calculate the right drug dose for that specific individual.
Nanomedicine Drug Delivery Systems: Nanomedicine wraps drugs inside tiny particles (1/1000th the width of a hair) to deliver them more effectively to diseased tissue while protecting healthy tissue.
Multi-Target Drug Pharmacology (Polypharmacology): For decades, drug design aimed for a "magic bullet" — one drug, one target, one disease.
Computational Drug Repurposing Methods: Drug repurposing uses computers to find new uses for drugs that are already approved and proven safe.

12_medical_sciences_pharmacology_princip.english: Covalent Drug Design and Targeted Electrophilic Pharmacology
12_medical_sciences_pharmacology_princip.text: Covalent Drug Design and Targeted Electrophilic Pharmacology: Most drugs work by temporarily sitting in a protein's binding pocket, like a key in a lock, and eventually falling out.
Radiopharmaceutical Theranostics: Radiopharmaceutical theranostics is the concept of "see it, treat it" --- using the same molecular targeting vehicle to both image and treat cancer.

12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.text: Planetary Health: Planetary health recognizes that human health ultimately depends on the health of Earth's natural systems.

12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.english: Wastewater Surveillance for Population Health Monitoring
12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.text: Wastewater Surveillance for Population Health Monitoring: Analyzing sewage provides a real-time, anonymous health check-up for an entire community.

Climate-Sensitive Disease Surveillance and Adaptation: Climate change is making heat waves deadlier, expanding the range of mosquito-borne diseases, and intensifying floods and droughts that threaten health.
One Health Framework: One Health recognizes that human health cannot be understood in isolation from animal health and the environment.

Social Determinants of Health Measurement: Social determinants of health are the conditions where people are born, grow, live, work, and age that powerfully shape their health outcomes — often more so than medical care itself.
Infodemic Management and Health Misinformation: Planetary health is the idea that human health and the health of the planet are inseparable.
Health Equity Measurement Frameworks: Health equity measurement puts numbers on unfairness.

12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.english: Syndemic Theory in Public Health Practice
12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.text: Syndemic Theory in Public Health Practice: A syndemic occurs when multiple health problems come together in a population and make each other worse, driven by shared social conditions.

12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.english: Implementation Science for Public Health Scale-Up
12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.text: Implementation Science for Public Health Scale-Up: Implementation science is about closing the enormous gap between what we know works in public health and what actually happens in communities.
Health Technology Assessment (HTA) Methodology: Health technology assessment answers a critical question: does this new treatment provide enough health benefit to justify its cost?

Equity-Focused Health Impact Assessment: Equity-focused health impact assessment asks: who benefits and who is harmed when a new policy is implemented, and are the benefits and harms distributed fairly across different groups in society?

12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.english: Climate Change Adaptation in Public Health Systems
12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.text: Climate Change Adaptation in Public Health Systems: Climate change is not just an environmental issue — it is a public health emergency.

Precision Public Health Genomics: Precision public health genomics uses genetic testing not just for individual patients, but for entire populations, to identify people at high risk of diseases like cancer, heart disease, or drug reactions before they ever get sick.

12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.english: Participatory Action Research in Community Health
12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.text: Participatory Action Research in Community Health: Participatory action research flips the traditional research model: instead of academic experts studying a community from the outside, PAR makes community members full partners in the research --- from deciding what questions to ask, to collecting data, to interpreting results, to taking action on findings.

12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.english: Wastewater-Based Epidemiology for Population Health Surveillance
12_medical_sciences_public_health_princi.text: Wastewater-Based Epidemiology for Population Health Surveillance: Wastewater-based epidemiology turns a city's sewer system into a giant diagnostic tool.

13_architecture_and_design_architecture_.text: Regenerative Architecture: Regenerative architecture goes beyond sustainability (doing less harm) to actively improving the environment.

13_architecture_and_design_architecture_.english: Biophilic Design: Nature-Human Connection in the Built Environment
13_architecture_and_design_architecture_.text: Biophilic Design: Nature-Human Connection in the Built Environment: Biophilic design brings nature into buildings based on the scientific finding that humans are healthier, happier, and more productive when connected to natural elements.

Circular Economy Principles in Architecture: Circular architecture treats buildings as temporary assemblies of valuable components rather than permanent structures destined for demolition and landfill.
