Passive Survivability: Passive survivability asks: if the power goes out for days or weeks — during a hurricane, an ice storm, or a heat wave — can this building keep its occupants alive without any electricity, gas, or running water?
Evidence-Based Design in Healthcare Architecture: Evidence-based design applies the same rigorous evidence standards used in medicine to the design of healthcare buildings.

Cradle-to-Cradle Material Cycles in Architecture: Instead of building everything on a muddy construction site exposed to weather and labor shortages, modular construction builds rooms, apartments, or entire floors in a factory — with precision, quality control, and no rain delays.
Computational Morphogenesis in Architecture: Computational morphogenesis lets architecture grow itself.

13_architecture_and_design_architecture_.english: Responsive Architecture and Kinetic Building Systems
13_architecture_and_design_architecture_.text: Responsive Architecture and Kinetic Building Systems: Responsive architecture means buildings that move and adapt, like living organisms responding to their environment.

Post-Occupancy Evaluation (POE) Methodology: Post-occupancy evaluation is the practice of going back to a building after it has been occupied and measuring whether it actually performs as the architects and engineers intended.
Hygrothermal Performance of Building Envelopes: Hygrothermal performance is about how heat and moisture move together through the walls and roof of a building.

13_architecture_and_design_architecture_.english: Thermal Comfort Theory (Fanger and Adaptive Models)
13_architecture_and_design_architecture_.text: Thermal Comfort Theory (Fanger and Adaptive Models): Thermal comfort theory predicts whether people will feel comfortable in a building based on measurable environmental conditions and what they are wearing and doing.

13_architecture_and_design_architecture_.english: Life Cycle Assessment in Building Design
13_architecture_and_design_architecture_.text: Life Cycle Assessment in Building Design: Life cycle assessment for buildings adds up all the environmental damage caused by a building from the moment raw materials are dug out of the ground to the moment the building is demolished and its materials are landfilled or recycled.

Daylighting Design Science: Daylighting design science brings natural light deep into buildings in a way that is comfortable (no glare), uniform (no dark corners), and sufficient (enough light to work without turning on electric lights for most of the day).

Architectural Acoustics and Room Tuning: Architectural acoustics is the science of how sound behaves in rooms --- what makes a concert hall sound magnificent, a classroom intelligible, and an open office tolerable.
Mass Timber Structural Systems: Mass timber construction uses large, engineered wood panels and beams to build tall buildings that rival the height and performance of concrete and steel structures.

13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.text: 4D Printing in Product Design: 4D printing adds the dimension of time to 3D-printed products.
Metamaterial Design for Functional Products: Metamaterial design creates products whose physical behavior comes from their internal geometric structure rather than the material itself.

13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.english: Digital Product Passport and Lifecycle Transparency
13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.text: Digital Product Passport and Lifecycle Transparency: A digital product passport is like a birth certificate and medical record for every product.
Affordance Theory in Product Design: Affordance theory explains why some products are immediately intuitive and others require an instruction manual.

Human-Centered Design Thinking Process: Design thinking is a structured process for innovation that starts with deeply understanding the people you are designing for, rather than starting with a solution or technology.

13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.english: Design for Longevity and Emotional Durability
13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.text: Design for Longevity and Emotional Durability: Most product design focuses on how things look, but people experience products with all their senses.
Speculative Design and Design Fiction: Speculative design creates products and experiences from imaginary futures to help us think about where we are headed and whether we want to go there.

13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.english: Transition Design for Systemic Societal Change
13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.text: Transition Design for Systemic Societal Change: Transition design tackles the biggest challenges facing society — climate change, inequality, food insecurity, public health crises — by using design to help communities and institutions shift from current unsustainable systems to sustainable ones.

13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.english: Design for Behavioral Change (Design for Sustainable Behavior)
13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.text: Design for Behavioral Change (Design for Sustainable Behavior): Design for behavioral change uses the design of products and systems to nudge, guide, or help people act more sustainably without relying on willpower or education alone.
Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA): DFMA asks: can we redesign this product so it has fewer parts, is easier to put together, and costs less to make — without sacrificing functionality?

Kansei Engineering (Affective Design): Kansei engineering answers the question: "How do we make a product feel elegant, powerful, playful, or luxurious?" It bridges the gap between the subjective, emotional reactions people have to products and the concrete design decisions (shape, color, material, texture) that create those feelings.

13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.english: Parametric and Generative Design for Products
13_architecture_and_design_industrial_de.text: Parametric and Generative Design for Products: Parametric and generative design lets the computer design products.

Co-Design and Participatory Design Methods: Co-design puts the people who will actually use a product, service, or system at the center of the design process --- not just as testers at the end, but as creative partners from the very beginning.
Speculative and Critical Design: Speculative and critical design creates provocative objects and scenarios that ask "what if?" rather than solving immediate practical problems.

Systems-Oriented Design for Complex Problems: Systems-oriented design recognizes that the most important design challenges --- climate change, healthcare inequality, food system transformation, urban sustainability --- are not product problems but system problems.

13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.text: Parametric Urbanism: Parametric urbanism uses computer algorithms to design cities and neighborhoods.

13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.english: Urban Digital Twin and Smart City Infrastructure
13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.text: Urban Digital Twin and Smart City Infrastructure: An urban digital twin is a living computer model of a city that mirrors what is happening in real time --- traffic flowing, energy being consumed, air quality changing, water systems operating.
Regenerative Urbanism and Nature-Based Solutions: Regenerative urbanism designs cities that heal the environment rather than just damaging it less.
Tactical Urbanism Theory and Practice: Tactical urbanism is the idea of trying out urban changes cheaply and temporarily before committing to expensive permanent construction.
Urban Food Systems Planning: Urban food systems planning recognizes that food is a spatial and infrastructure issue, not just an agricultural one.
Age-Friendly City Design: Cities face increasing heat waves, flooding, droughts, and storms because of climate change.
Complete Streets Design: Complete streets are designed for everyone, not just cars.
Food-Sensitive Urban Design: Food-sensitive urban design recognizes that where people live determines what they eat, which determines their health.

13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.english: Tactical Urbanism and Incremental Urban Transformation
13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.text: Tactical Urbanism and Incremental Urban Transformation: Tactical urbanism is the practice of making quick, cheap, temporary changes to streets and public spaces to test whether a design idea actually works before spending millions on permanent construction.

13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.english: Urban Commons and Shared Space Governance
13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.text: Urban Commons and Shared Space Governance: Urban commons are shared resources in cities — parks, community gardens, coworking spaces, shared streets, local waterways — that belong to everyone and require collective management to avoid degradation.
Stormwater Management and Low-Impact Development: Low-impact development manages rainwater where it falls rather than piping it away through storm drains.

13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.english: Urban Freight and Last-Mile Logistics Planning
13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.text: Urban Freight and Last-Mile Logistics Planning: Urban freight planning addresses the trucks, vans, and delivery vehicles that keep cities supplied with goods — from restaurant ingredients to e-commerce parcels to construction materials.

13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.english: Smart City Digital Infrastructure and Data Governance
13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.text: Smart City Digital Infrastructure and Data Governance: Smart city infrastructure uses sensors, data, and algorithms to make cities work better --- managing traffic flow in real time, predicting where flooding will occur, optimizing garbage collection routes, and monitoring air quality block by block.

Urban Heat Island Mitigation Strategies: Cities are hotter than the countryside because dark roofs and roads absorb sunlight, concrete and asphalt store heat during the day and release it at night, there are fewer trees and less soil to provide cooling evaporation, and buildings and vehicles pump out waste heat.

13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.english: Participatory Digital Planning and Civic Technology
13_architecture_and_design_urban_plannin.text: Participatory Digital Planning and Civic Technology: Participatory digital planning brings public engagement in planning decisions online, reaching thousands of residents instead of the dozens who attend public meetings.

14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.text: CRISPR-Based Crop Engineering: CRISPR is a molecular scissors tool that lets plant breeders make precise changes to a crop's DNA --- knocking out genes that make plants susceptible to disease, activating genes that improve nutritional quality, or fine-tuning genes that control flowering time.

14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.english: Vertical Farming: Controlled Environment Agriculture Physics
14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.text: Vertical Farming: Controlled Environment Agriculture Physics: Vertical farms grow crops indoors in stacked layers under LED lights, without soil and with precisely controlled temperature, humidity, and nutrients.

14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.english: Regenerative Grazing and Soil Carbon Sequestration
14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.text: Regenerative Grazing and Soil Carbon Sequestration: Regenerative grazing uses cattle and other livestock as land management tools, mimicking the movement patterns of wild herds.
Allelopathy in Crop Systems: Allelopathy is chemical warfare among plants.

Biofortification: Biofortification means breeding staple crops — rice, wheat, beans, sweet potato, pearl millet — to contain more vitamins and minerals so that people eating their normal diets get better nutrition without changing their food habits.
Silvopasture: Integrated Tree-Livestock Systems: Controlled environment agriculture grows crops indoors under precisely controlled conditions — think of a factory for lettuce, herbs, or strawberries.
Conservation Agriculture and No-Till Systems: Conservation agriculture means farming without plowing.

14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.english: Deficit Irrigation and Regulated Water Stress
14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.text: Deficit Irrigation and Regulated Water Stress: Deficit irrigation is the counterintuitive strategy of deliberately giving crops less water than they need at specific times.

14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.english: Cover Crop Ecology and Multi-Functional Services
14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.text: Cover Crop Ecology and Multi-Functional Services: Cover crops are planted not to harvest but to protect and improve the soil between cash crop seasons.
Crop-Livestock Integration Systems: Crop-livestock integration puts animals and crops on the same farm — not as separate enterprises but as partners in a cycle.

14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.english: Biological Pest Control and Conservation Biocontrol
14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.text: Biological Pest Control and Conservation Biocontrol: Biological pest control uses nature's own pest killers — predators, parasites, and pathogens — to keep crop pests in check without synthetic pesticides.

14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.english: Plant Phenotyping and High-Throughput Field Screening
14_agricultural_sciences_agronomy_princi.text: Plant Phenotyping and High-Throughput Field Screening: Plant phenotyping measures the physical traits of crops — how tall they grow, how much leaf area they produce, how quickly they mature, how efficiently they photosynthesize, how well they tolerate drought.

Intercropping Systems Ecology and Overyielding: Intercropping grows two or more crops together on the same field --- such as maize with beans, wheat with clover, or cassava with cowpea --- and typically produces more total food per hectare than growing either crop alone.

Plant Growth-Promoting Rhizobacteria (PGPR): Plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria are beneficial bacteria that live on and around plant roots, acting as natural fertilizer factories, disease protectors, and growth stimulators.

Pollinator-Dependent Crop Management: Many of the world's most important food crops --- almonds, apples, blueberries, coffee, cacao, watermelons, and dozens more --- depend on bees, butterflies, and other pollinators to transfer pollen and produce fruit.

14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.text: Precision Fermentation for Food Ingredients: Precision fermentation is like brewing beer, but instead of making alcohol, the microorganisms are programmed to make specific food ingredients --- milk proteins without cows, egg proteins without chickens, or collagen without animals.

14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.english: CRISPR Applications in Food Crop Improvement
14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.text: CRISPR Applications in Food Crop Improvement: CRISPR gene editing lets food scientists precisely modify the genes of food crops to improve nutritional value, extend shelf life, reduce allergens, and enhance processing quality --- all without adding foreign DNA.

14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.english: Food Systems Lifecycle Assessment and Planetary Boundaries
14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.text: Food Systems Lifecycle Assessment and Planetary Boundaries: Food LCA tracks the total environmental footprint of what we eat, from farm to fork to landfill.

14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.english: Food Matrix Effects on Nutrient Bioavailability
14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.text: Food Matrix Effects on Nutrient Bioavailability: The same nutrient can behave completely differently depending on the food it comes in.
Maillard Reaction Kinetics and Control: The Maillard reaction is what makes bread crusts golden, coffee aromatic, grilled steak flavorful, and chocolate brown.

High-Pressure Processing (HPP) Thermodynamics: The world needs more protein for a growing population, but producing that protein primarily from animals — especially beef and dairy — uses too much land, water, and energy and produces too many greenhouse gas emissions.

14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.english: Fermentation Biotechnology and Starter Culture Engineering
14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.text: Fermentation Biotechnology and Starter Culture Engineering: Fermentation is humanity's oldest biotechnology — turning milk into cheese, grapes into wine, cabbage into kimchi — and it is now a precise science.
Food Nanotechnology and Nanoencapsulation: Food nanotechnology works at the scale of billionths of a meter to solve practical food problems.

Food Authenticity and Fraud Detection: Food fraud is a multi-billion-dollar global problem: olive oil diluted with cheaper seed oils, honey adulterated with sugar syrup, fish mislabeled as a more expensive species, organic products that are actually conventionally grown.

14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.english: Non-Thermal Plasma Processing for Food Safety
14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.text: Non-Thermal Plasma Processing for Food Safety: Cold plasma processing uses electrically charged gas — a soup of reactive molecules, UV light, and charged particles — to kill bacteria, molds, and viruses on food surfaces without heating the food.
Encapsulation Technology for Bioactive Ingredients: Encapsulation wraps sensitive food ingredients inside tiny protective shells — like putting a vitamin pill inside an even tinier capsule.

14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.english: Predictive Microbiology and Quantitative Risk Assessment
14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.text: Predictive Microbiology and Quantitative Risk Assessment: Predictive microbiology uses mathematical models to forecast what harmful bacteria will do in food under different conditions — how fast they will grow at a given temperature, how long they survive in an acidic marinade, how quickly they are killed during cooking.

14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.english: Ohmic Heating for Continuous Food Processing
14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.text: Ohmic Heating for Continuous Food Processing: Ohmic heating cooks food by passing electricity directly through it — the food itself becomes the heating element.

Supercritical CO2 Extraction Technology: Supercritical CO2 extraction uses carbon dioxide in a special state — heated and pressurized beyond its critical point — as a solvent to pull desired compounds out of food.

14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.english: Cellular Agriculture and Cultured Meat Science
14_agricultural_sciences_food_science_pr.text: Cellular Agriculture and Cultured Meat Science: Cultured meat grows real animal meat from cells in a bioreactor instead of raising and slaughtering whole animals.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Agroforestry Carbon Accounting and Climate Mitigation
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Agroforestry Carbon Accounting and Climate Mitigation: Agroforestry --- growing trees alongside crops or livestock --- is one of the most effective land-based strategies for capturing carbon from the atmosphere.
Forest Microbiome and Tree Health: Every forest tree lives in intimate partnership with billions of microorganisms.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Forest-Based Bioeconomy and Mass Timber Engineering
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Forest-Based Bioeconomy and Mass Timber Engineering: The forest bioeconomy uses wood from sustainably managed forests to replace carbon-intensive materials like steel, concrete, and plastic.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Seed Bank and Seed Ecology in Forest Regeneration
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Seed Bank and Seed Ecology in Forest Regeneration: Forest regeneration after fire, logging, or windstorm depends on where seeds come from and whether they find suitable places to grow.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Mass Timber Engineering and Cross-Laminated Timber
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Mass Timber Engineering and Cross-Laminated Timber: Mass timber is a revolution in construction: building tall buildings — 10, 15, even 25 stories — primarily from engineered wood instead of concrete and steel.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Mycorrhizal Networks in Forest Ecosystem Function
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Mycorrhizal Networks in Forest Ecosystem Function: Drones are transforming how forests are monitored and managed.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Wood Modification Technology and Thermally Modified Timber
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Wood Modification Technology and Thermally Modified Timber: Wood is an excellent building material but it rots, swells, shrinks, and gets eaten by fungi and insects when it gets wet.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Urban Forestry and Canopy Ecosystem Services
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Urban Forestry and Canopy Ecosystem Services: Urban trees are not just decorative — they are critical infrastructure.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Forest Bioeconomy and Non-Timber Forest Products
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Forest Bioeconomy and Non-Timber Forest Products: Forests produce far more than lumber.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Forest Carbon Flux Measurement and Eddy Covariance
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Forest Carbon Flux Measurement and Eddy Covariance: Eddy covariance measures how much carbon dioxide a forest breathes in and out, minute by minute, for years at a time.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Assisted Natural Regeneration and Close-to-Nature Silviculture
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Assisted Natural Regeneration and Close-to-Nature Silviculture: Assisted natural regeneration restores forests by working with nature rather than against it.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Forest Landscape Restoration and the Bonn Challenge
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Forest Landscape Restoration and the Bonn Challenge: Forest landscape restoration rebuilds degraded forests and the benefits they provide — clean water, carbon storage, biodiversity, timber, and livelihoods — across entire landscapes.
Dendroecology and Tree-Ring Science: Dendroecology reads the history of forests by studying tree rings.

Community Forestry Governance and Tenure: Community forestry governance is about local communities managing their own forests — making and enforcing the rules for who can use the forest, what can be harvested, and how the forest is protected.

14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.english: Forest Microbiome and Soil-Tree Feedback Systems
14_agricultural_sciences_forestry_princi.text: Forest Microbiome and Soil-Tree Feedback Systems: The forest microbiome is the invisible ecosystem of microorganisms — fungi, bacteria, and other microbes — that lives in and on every part of a forest, from the soil to the treetops.

14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.text: Soil Microbiome Engineering: Soil microbiome engineering is the deliberate manipulation of the trillions of microorganisms living in soil to help crops grow better, resist disease, and capture more nutrients.
Soil Carbon Measurement and Verification: Measuring how much carbon is stored in soil sounds simple but is actually one of agriculture's hardest measurement challenges.
Soil-Climate Feedback Mechanisms: Soils are both victims and amplifiers of climate change.

Mycorrhizal Network Ecology: Beneath the soil surface, the roots of different plants are often connected by a vast network of fungal threads — mycorrhizal fungi that form partnerships with plant roots.
Soil Aggregate Hierarchy Theory: Soil is not a random pile of particles — it is organized into a hierarchy of clumps within clumps, like Russian nesting dolls.
Seed Bank Ecology and Persistence: Plants change the soil they grow in, and those changes affect what can grow there next.
Phytoremediation and Plant-Soil Contaminant Interactions: Phytoremediation uses plants as living cleanup machines for contaminated soil.

14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.english: Pedotransfer Functions and Digital Soil Mapping
14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.text: Pedotransfer Functions and Digital Soil Mapping: Soil scientists cannot measure every property of every soil everywhere — it would take centuries and cost billions.

Soil Salinization Processes and Reclamation: When salts build up in soil — from irrigation water, rising salty groundwater, or natural processes in dry climates — the soil becomes saline, and most crops cannot grow.

14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.english: Soil Microbial Ecology and Functional Guilds
14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.text: Soil Microbial Ecology and Functional Guilds: A single teaspoon of healthy soil contains more microorganisms than there are people on Earth — billions of bacteria, fungi, archaea, and other tiny life forms.

14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.english: Soil Organic Carbon Fractionation and Turnover
14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.text: Soil Organic Carbon Fractionation and Turnover: Not all soil carbon is equal.
Soil-Groundwater Interactions and Contaminant Transport: Soil-groundwater interactions are about how water and pollutants move downward through soil to reach the groundwater we drink.
Biochar Soil Amendment Science: Biochar is essentially charcoal made from plant waste that is mixed into soil as an amendment.

14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.english: Soil Crust Biology and Dryland Soil Surface Ecology
14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.text: Soil Crust Biology and Dryland Soil Surface Ecology: Biological soil crusts are the living skin of desert soils — a thin, fragile layer of tiny organisms (cyanobacteria, mosses, lichens) that form a community on the soil surface.

14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.english: Electrochemical Soil Sensing and Real-Time Nutrient Monitoring
14_agricultural_sciences_soil_science_pr.text: Electrochemical Soil Sensing and Real-Time Nutrient Monitoring: Electrochemical soil sensors work like tiny chemical laboratories buried in the soil, continuously measuring nutrient levels, pH, and salinity without needing to collect soil samples and send them to a lab.
